issue_title: Allow parsing subs with no ending blank line
issue_body: Many sub editors don't add a blank line to the end, and many editors accept it. We should just accept this too in input.

issue_title: Trailing new lines are stripped or mangled
issue_body:
test code 

```
import toml

content = r"""
[test]

multi_line = "some lines\n\n"

"""

config = toml.loads(content)

print repr(config['test']['multi_line'])

```
Actual result: `u'some lines\n\\n'`
Expected result: `u'some lines\n\n'`

See the extra `\`

-------------

I get wrong errors if I am using values like

```

multi_line = "some lines\r\n"
multi_line = "some lines\r\n\r\n"

```

-----------

I have also tried this code

```
import toml

content = (
    '[test]\n'
    'multi_line = """some lines\n'
    '\n'
    '\n'
    '\n'
    '"""'
    )

config = toml.loads(content)

print repr(config['test']['multi_line'])
```

Actual result: `u'some lines\n'`
Expected result: `u'some lines\n\n\n\n'`

issue_title: Failing to connect to Freenode
issue_body:
I couldn't figure out why the library wouldn't connect, and then I tried the builtin `testbot.py` script.

`python testbot.py irc.freenode.net:6697 #<channel> testbot19630` doesn't work. 

By throwing some q() logs in there it looks like the connection's sockets are lost...

issue_title: gmpy2 and numpy 'O' arrays
issue_body:
This is not really a gmpy2 issue (already [reported in numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/7402)), but it may be relevant to some gmpy2 users. In fact I stumbled on this while using gmpy2.

I was replacing Python `long` integers in a numpy 'O' array with `mpz` and `np.cumsum` started giving wrong results. I have also tried putting `mpfr` inside numpy 'O' arrays, and the results are also wrong, though in a different way. My usage scenario might not be standard, but I could imagine someone replacing `float` with `mpfr` in an array to gain accuracy in favor of speed.

Here is a minimal code:
```python
import numpy as np
from gmpy2 import mpz, mpfr

# example with mpfr
arr = np.empty(4, dtype=object)
arr[:] = [mpfr('1.0') for _ in range(4)]
print arr
# arr = np.cumsum(arr)  # works
np.cumsum(arr, out=arr)  # wrong
print arr

# example with mpz
arr = np.empty(4, dtype=object)
arr[:] = [mpz(1) for _ in range(4)]
print arr
# arr = np.cumsum(arr)  # works
np.cumsum(arr, out=arr)  #wrong
print arr
np.cumsum(arr, out=arr)
print arr

# example with Python list
arr = np.empty(4, dtype=object)
arr[:] = [[1] for _ in range(4)]
print arr
# arr = np.cumsum(arr)  # works
np.cumsum(arr, out=arr)
print arr
```
If Python `list` is replaced with `tuple` in the last example, then the code works. So it seems that mutability of `mpfr` and `mpz` could be somehow involved.

issue_title: np.isfinite() changes internal type?
issue_body:
I have no idea what's going on here:
```python
import numpy as np
from pint import UnitRegistry
ureg = UnitRegistry()
a = np.arange(2.)
au = a * ureg.m

# Numpy Arrays
print('Numpy (base):', [type(v) for v in a])
print('Numpy (isfinite):', [type(v) for v in a if np.isfinite(v)])

# Quantity
print('Pint (base):', [type(v.magnitude) for v in au]) # ok
print('Pint (isfinite):', [type(v.magnitude) for v in au if np.isfinite(v)]) # Huh?
```
Outputs:
```
Numpy (base): [<class 'numpy.float64'>, <class 'numpy.float64'>]
Numpy (isfinite): [<class 'numpy.float64'>, <class 'numpy.float64'>]
Pint (base): [<class 'numpy.float64'>, <class 'numpy.float64'>]
Pint (isfinite): [<class 'numpy.ndarray'>, <class 'numpy.ndarray'>]
```
Why on earth does calling a numpy function modify the Quantity *in-place*???

The reason this even matters is because this works:
```python

```

issue_title: Barrel vs Barrel
issue_body:
I noticed that barrel is using US Oil Barrel size of 42 US gallons. It's probably more common to convert barrels to other units when talking about beer, e.g. # of US pints in a 1/2 bbl (US beer barrel). 

It might be worth splitting barrel into:
  us_oil_barrel = 42 * gallon
  us_liquid_barrel = 31.5 * gallon
  us_beer_barrel = 31 * gallon
  imperial_barrel = 36 * imperial_gallon

See:
  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barrel_(unit)

issue_title: No SQ lines
Segmented inverted alignments
issue_body:
I'm looking at an example where a read has an inversion in the middle. Everything gets aligned properly, but I do not understand why the inverted part has its cigar reversed. The flag only states 2064, for supplements and reverse. Is this a special combination, leading to different record printing? Do you have documentation/specs about this case?

Examples:
Reference https://gist.github.com/armintoepfer/ef6505a5a9db5c1ba9b49ffe910b326e
Seqs: https://gist.github.com/armintoepfer/a24959b2e199784c35fc11651aee84d9

    minimap2 -Y -x map-pb -a -z 400,20 ref.fasta seqs.fasta

## First example:
The first sequence `mid_inv` should be aligned in three segments `180 fwd, 540 rev, 1380 fwd`.
The longest is picked as primary,

    mid_inv 0 chr02__substr__128948_131048 721 60 720S1380M

Makes totally sense.
The first part also gets aligned as forward and supplementary

    mid_inv 2048 chr02__substr__128948_131048 1 60 180M1920S

Now the confusion for the middle part

    mid_inv 2064 chr02__substr__128948_131048 181 0 1380S540M180S

The cigar is reverse and the actual SEQ is also reverse-complemented.

## Second example `end_inv`
Here, the sequence is split into two segments `960 fwd, 1140 rev`.
Minimap2 picks the second segment as the primary alignment, reversed the cigar and rc the SEQ

    end_inv 16 chr02__substr__128948_131048 961 60 1140M960S

And the first part is aligned properly as forward

    end_inv 2048 chr02__substr__128948_131048 1 60 960M1140S

But if I align a single segment that is reverse-complement, the cigar and sequence are forward strand (genomic) with flag 16 for rev.

Why are segments treated differently? And why are the inversions tagged with MAPQ 0?
Using 2.10-r763-dirty

issue_title: Add support for the XDG Base Directory Specification
issue_body:
This [blog post](https://ploum.net/207-modify-your-application-to-use-xdg-folders/) lists the advantages of using the XDG Base Directory Specification as follows:
- $HOME is a lot less cluttered.
- Backups are a lot more safer and easier. (you know that backuping your $XDG_DATA_HOME along with your files is enough)
- A lot easier to reset a default configuration if you want/need it (and without any risk of loosing information).
- Avoid some strange bugs that happen because you had a old version of some configuration file.
- It is a lot more flexible and portable because no paths are hard-coded. You can use the XDG library that does the job for you _or_, if you don’t want the dependency, implementing the XDG specification is only a few lines of code.

There's currently the PR #160 which is waiting for you take action on.

x, y, z, t = symbols('x y z t')
```
Although I'm not a big fan of determining the names from the string ```x y z t```, symfit should have something similar:
```python
x, y = variables('x y')
a, b = parameters('a b')
```
Or if you use the ```inspect``` for DRY philosophy used in single Argument definitions,
```python
x, y = variables()
a, b = parameters()
```
The function itself would be a oneliner in the first case:
```python
def variables(names):
    return [Variable(name=name) for name in names.split()]
```

issue_title: pysha3 uses private 'pystrhex.h' API
issue_body: According to PyPy upstream (https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/2687/pypy3-is-missing-pystrhexh-breaking-pysha3) pystrhex.h is a private API of CPython and should not be used in the extensions. I would therefore suggest removing the conditional that uses it and always use the local implementation.

I can't find any other comments on this, so I have to assume it's unique to my setup or it's by design and I missed it in the documentation somewhere.

I'm using Windows 10 with Python 3.6.5, wsgidav 2.4.0.
When I run `wsgidav --host=0.0.0.0 --port=8080 --root=c:\users\my_user` :

I can connect to the share without problems, view and download files without problems. But when I upload a file to the webdav share (I use drag-drop), it gets a new timestamp of "now". Strangely the new file initially shows its original timestamp, but a refresh (F5) will show the new "now" timestamp.

Is this by design? It's hard for me to believe that it is because it makes any sort of file sync impossible.

NB: I also see this same behavior in SeaFile, which uses the wsgidav library.

Edit: I also tried using a config file and setting `mutable_live_props = ["{DAV:}getlastmodified"]` which didn't help. So I'm guessing this it's the client not setting "lastmodified" after the upload?

issue_title: leafref among different submodules
IPython adds spurious members to addicts
issue_body:
This isn't really a problem with Addict, but it might deserve a warning or to be a special case. 

IPython touches objects with getattr when working interactively to determine their nature. This means that you get the following: 
```

In [1]: import addict 

In [2]: d = addict.Dict()

In [3]: d
Out[3]: {'_ipython_canary_method_should_not_exist_': {}}
```
due to the way that Addict overloads getattr. 

Is there a chance that we could address this somehow? It's a particular matter because one of my biggest reasons for using Addict is the interactive value.

issue_title: Header only?
issue_body:
Hey @davisking!

I'm considering to change the cmake again a little, such that we can disable building both the static and the shared library. Because, for now, my main use is the face detector, which is header-only. But the entire dlib project is build, twice (static + shared), when it's totally unnecessary.

Any recommendations or thoughts? I'd go with two CMake options: `DLIB_BUILD_STATIC`, `DLIB_BUILD_SHARED`, which have default values that correspond to what we have now. This would allow to disable both, resulting in CMake simply making an `INTERFACE` library with the necessary include paths and build flags.

issue_title: Make sure Windows wheels don't have `py27m` in them
issue_body:
Currently, we [see][4] the not-so-fun warning ([also][5]):

```
C:\Python36-x64\lib\site-packages\wheel\pep425tags.py:75: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'Py_DEBUG' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
C:\Python36-x64\lib\site-packages\wheel\pep425tags.py:79: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'WITH_PYMALLOC' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
```

and

```
C:\Python27-x64\lib\site-packages\wheel\pep425tags.py:75: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'Py_DEBUG' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
C:\Python27-x64\lib\site-packages\wheel\pep425tags.py:79: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'WITH_PYMALLOC' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
C:\Python27-x64\lib\site-packages\wheel\pep425tags.py:85: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'Py_UNICODE_SIZE' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  sys.version_info < (3, 3))) \
```

and end up with wheel names like:

```
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp27-cp27m-win32.whl
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp27-cp27m-win_amd64.whl
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp35-cp35m-win32.whl
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp35-cp35m-win_amd64.whl
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp36-cp36m-win32.whl
bezier-0.5.0.dev1-cp36-cp36m-win_amd64.whl
```

Comparing to what [`numpy` does][3]:

```
numpy-1.13.3-2-cp27-none-win32.whl
numpy-1.13.3-cp27-none-win_amd64.whl
numpy-1.13.3-2-cp34-none-win32.whl
numpy-1.13.3-cp34-none-win_amd64.whl
numpy-1.13.3-2-cp35-none-win32.whl
numpy-1.13.3-cp35-none-win_amd64.whl
numpy-1.13.3-2-cp36-none-win32.whl
numpy-1.13.3-cp36-none-win_amd64.whl
```

Just as we have a [custom `build_ext`][1], I could add a [custom `bdist_wheel`][2]:

```python
import distutils.util

import wheel.bdist_wheel.bdist_wheel


class CustomBdistWheel(wheel.bdist_wheel.bdist_wheel):

    def finalize_options(self):
        wheel.bdist_wheel.bdist_wheel.finalize_options(self)
        # self.universal = True
        self.plat_name_supplied = True
        self.plat_name = distutils.util.get_platform()


setup(cmdclass={'bdist_wheel': CustomBdistWheel})
```

[1]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/db0cfb5b4852573e3bb937d634c5db203bbd1523/setup_helpers.py#L304
[2]: https://github.com/pypa/wheel/issues/161
[3]: https://pypi.org/project/numpy/1.13.3/#files
[4]: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/dhermes/bezier-wheels/build/1.0.69/job/xnsufff562a9a9yk
[5]: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/dhermes/bezier-wheels/build/1.0.69/job/tbs80x93fde1scad

issue_title: non-numeric expiration claim does not raise an error
issue_body:
I would expect a non-numeric expiration claim to raise an error but it does not. To reproduce, here's a JWT with ``{exp: '<not a number>'}``':

````
>>> import jwt
>>> import calendar, time
>>> iat = calendar.timegm(time.gmtime())
>>> token = jwt.encode({'aud': 'some-aud', 'iss': 'some-iss', 'typ': 'some-typ', 'iat': iat, 'exp': '<not a number>', 'request': {}}, 'secret')
>>> jwt.decode(token, 'secret', verify=True, audience='some-aud')
{u'aud': u'some-aud', u'iss': u'some-iss', u'request': {}, u'exp': u'<not a number>', u'iat': 1427495823, u'typ': u'some-typ'}
````

If a signature checking was somehow bypassed, an expiration like this could maybe allow for replay attacks.

Especially it is important since this library in most of the cases imply commercial and proprietary use, and you definitely don't want to have any liabilities to those who use your code ;-) By default with no-license your code is protected by the copyright, so: "This means that you retain all rights to your source code and that nobody else may reproduce, distribute, or create derivative works from your work." 

Unfortunately I could not advise you much here - I'm not a legal specialist. But you can have a look at a rather good summary here: https://tldrlegal.com/ or here: http://choosealicense.com/. Also there's a FAQ here: https://help.github.com/articles/open-source-licensing/.

Personally, I prefer non-restrictive open-source licenses, and license my code under [MIT license](https://tldrlegal.com/license/mit-license). One of the drawback of restrictive GPL-type of licenses is a requirement to keep the license type and pass source code together with binaries when distributing, which often might be a "no-go" for financial applications.

Best regards
Vladimir

issue_title: Assuring password contains punctuation
issue_body:
When supplying limit_punctuation to password generation, is is not guaranteed that the punctuation will be included in the resulting password. This could be classified as a feature or a bug not sure which a user may consider. 

For now, if you want to assure a password contains the special characters from a limited list, you can use this small function. I will likely make an updated patch that does just this shortly.

```
def random_password(length, type='strong'):
    limit_punctuation="!@#$"
    while True:
        password = passgen(length=length, punctuation=True, limit_punctuation=limit_punctuation)
        print(password)
        if any(char in limit_punctuation for char in password):
            break
    return password
```

issue_title: ValueError: cannot create object arrays from iterator
issue_body:
The following code snippet raises a ValueError:
```python
import numpy as np
from pyodesys.symbolic import SymbolicSys

def rhs(f, g, u, c, k):
    """
    Make the rhs for coupled, controlled oscillators.
    .. math::
        \dot{x}_i = f(x_i) + \sum_j c_{ij} g(x_i, x_j) + k_iu(x_1,\ldots,x_n)

    :param f: onsite dynamics
    :param g: coupling function
    :param u: control function
    :param c: coupling matrix
    :param k: control switch
    :return: rhs
    """
    def inner(t, y, p):
        coupling = (sum(cij*g(xi, xj) for cij, xj in zip(ci,y)) for ci, xi in zip(c,y))
        coupling = np.fromiter(coupling, y.dtype)
        return f(y) + coupling + k*u(y)
    return inner

n = 3
f = rhs(lambda x: -x, lambda x, y: (x-y)**2, lambda x: 0, np.eye(n), np.zeros(n))

odesys = SymbolicSys.from_callback(f, n)
```

It works just fine if you use a list expression and `coupling = np.array(coupling)` tough.

issue_title: Autoformat of boolean equality comparison to True/False could change semantics
issue_body:
Hi! Apologies if this is not the correct place for this discussion, but I wanted to inform you about an issue we (https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore) had recently, related to autopep8, that you might want to take into consideration. It is not _necessarily_ a bug.

autopep8 will simplify boolean equality comparisons to a True/False literal, e.g.

`var == True` will be formatted to just `var`. However, if `var` is an object which overloads the `==` operator, this autoformat could result in changed semantics for the autoformatted program, depending on the `__eq__` overloading function.

Specifically for our use case, we use this style of operator overloading to conveniently create AST-like expression tree data structures. For example, `var == True`, would return an `EqualExpression` object. With the autoformat, this would be simplified to just `var`, which would be incorrect in the context of our project.

Due to the fact that this autoformat can sometimes cause bugs in the formatted program, perhaps we could consider not enabling this autoformat by default?

issue_title: 情感分析并不能判断“不高兴”这类情感
issue_body:
看了下源码，使用的是贝叶斯分类，完全依靠于词出现的概率。
但是在测试过程中遇到的问题是， “我今天不高兴” 会被判定成正向情感（sentiment>0.5)。因为分词会把不高兴分成不和高兴，这样一来高兴更多地出现在正向情感中而不并不能使之的情感发生转变。所有的“不高兴” “不快乐”，“不生气”“不难过” 都会产生相反的情感值。
而且当副词出现的时候，并不能有效地使情感值更极端，例如“我今天生气” 和“我今天很生气”和“我今天非常生气”的情感值虽然说不一样，但是也并没有和中文相对应的情感倾向吻合。

这样的问题应该怎么解决呢

issue_title: Comply with E402: module level import not at top of file
issue_body:
[migrate.py:15-23](https://github.com/kofrasa/migrate/blob/0.3.7/migrate.py#L15)
According to PEP8, all module level imports should be on top of the file.

See [Code lay-out](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#imports):
> Imports are always put at the top of the file, just after any module comments and docstrings, and before module globals and constants.
> 
> Imports should be grouped in the following order:
> 
>     standard library imports
>     related third party imports
>     local application/library specific imports
> 
> You should put a blank line between each group of imports.
> 
> Put any relevant `__all__` specification after the imports.
>

issue_title: Option to skip verify space for SCP function
issue_body:
Was wondering the LoE to add an option to skip the verify space function being automatically run when the file_transfer function is called.  Reason being is, a lot of devices (mostly terminal servers I'm seeing) that are backend unix/linux, they mount their temporary filesystems straight from memory, not from a disk.  The verify space is getting false data because the filesystem it's checking is returning all 0's for space available, used, blocks, etc.
Thoughts?

issue_title: have netmiko installed but cant run the scripts
unified Tor launcher/builder API
issue_body:
After discussions with @warner (thanks!) I have been convinced that a "higher level" API that abstracts/represents "a running Tor" is a good idea. This instance would be returned from "connect to tor" or "launch a new tor" factory-functions, and give access to the TorControlProtocol instance.

On top of that, it acts as a Factory/Builder for other interesting instances: TorState, TorConfig, client- and server- side endpoints, etc.

There is an early prototype in the "control" branch: https://github.com/meejah/txtorcon/tree/controller

issue_title: non unicode data not correctly handled in Python 3
issue_body: Filenames and command line arguments can include (non-unicode) binary data on most operating systems.  This is more difficult to handle on Python 3 because these are generally expressed as the str type.  I've created pull request https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/733 with a test case that highlights the issue but do not have a solution at this point.

issue_title: Process.exe() on FreeBSD may raise OSError(ENOENT)
issue_body:
```
======================================================================
FAIL: test_fetch_all (psutil.tests.test_process.TestFetchAllProcesses)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "psutil/tests/test_process.py", line 1605, in test_fetch_all
    self.fail(''.join(failures))
AssertionError:     
    ======================================================================
    FAIL: test_exe (proc=psutil.Process(pid=4018, name='screen'))
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "psutil/tests/test_process.py", line 1570, in test_fetch_all
        ret = attr(*args)
      File "psutil/__init__.py", line 605, in exe
        exe = self._proc.exe()
      File "psutil/_psbsd.py", line 413, in wrapper
        return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "psutil/_psbsd.py", line 461, in exe
        return cext.proc_exe(self.pid)
    OSError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory
```

The error originates from `sysctl` used with `KERN_PROC_PATHNAME`. Also `procstat` fails, although with a different error:

```
user@freebsd~/psutil$ sudo procstat -b 4108
procstat: sysctl(kern.proc): No such process
procstat: procstat_getprocs()
```

What we can do is return an empty string instead, since this is already allowed by exe() API.

issue_title: Support git bisect workflow to find perf regression
issue_body:
To find out which commit introduced a performance regression:

Add a sub-command that can be used with `git bisect` in a Crate repo to
automatically have it build the current checked out Crate version and then run
a spec file against that build.

After each run prompt to check if the result is good or bad.
Optionally a avg duration threshold could be used to decide automatically.

issue_title: Result checksum / verification option
issue_body:
Having an option that adds a result-checksum to the result and also verifies the checksum against different results if there is more than 1 iteration would be nice.
This would be useful for queries that produce deterministic results - to make sure there are no regressions introduced that produce garbage output.

issue_title: Race condition with save/delete (tested on 0.32.2)?
issue_body:
Here is a situation in which rom will save an incomplete object in redis and then later throw an exception because required columns are missing in the data store.

* Process A creates a object, let's say it has id 9
* Process B fetches data for id 9 and inflates it into an object
* Process B modifies a property of the object
* Process A calls delete() on object (id:9)
* Process B calls save() on object (id:9)
* Another get() is called for id 9 at a later time throws an exception since the incomplete object was saved back to redis

It seems like rom should not allow you to save() an object that has already had delete() called elsewhere. Or maybe it's not rom's problem to be thread safe, and it's up to me to make sure I don't run into these race conditions... not sure, but I thought I would at least throw out this sample/test code and get some input.

Thoughts?

```
import time
import rom
import threading

from nose import *

class Thing(rom.Model):
    a = rom.String(required=True, index=True, keygen=rom.SIMPLE)
    b = rom.String(default='foo', index=True, keygen=rom.SIMPLE)


def get_obj_wait_then_save(ev, i):
    o = Thing.get(i)

    while not ev.isSet():
        pass

    o.b = 'bar'
    o.save()


def test_race_condition():
    o = Thing(a='import value')
    assert o.save()

    f_id = o.id

    e = threading.Event()
    t = threading.Thread(name='non-block',
                         target=get_obj_wait_then_save,
                         args=(e, o.id))

    t.start()
    time.sleep(.01)

    o.delete()
    assert not Thing.get(f_id)

    e.set()
    time.sleep(.01)

    assert not Thing.get(f_id)
```
The failed test output:
```
E
======================================================================
ERROR: rom_thread_race.test_race_condition
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jmills/virtualenv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nose/case.py", line 197, in runTest
    self.test(*self.arg)
  File "/Users/jmills/rom_thread_race.py", line 42, in test_race_condition
    assert not Thing.get(f_id)
  File "/Users/jmills/virtualenv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/rom/model.py", line 565, in get
    out[i] = cls(_loading=True, **data)
  File "/Users/jmills/virtualenv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/rom/model.py", line 230, in __init__

    self._init_(obj, *value)
  File "/Users/jmills/virtualenv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/rom/columns.py", line 270, in _init_
    raise MissingColumn("%s.%s cannot be missing"%(self._model, self._attr))
MissingColumn: Thing.a cannot be missing

----------------------------------------------------------------------

issue_title: move all `required` logic into a separate class variable
issue_body:
some `required` parts of providers' schema can be more complex then a simple key list. In such cases, there's a need to override `Provider.required` property method and return the relevant part.

This should be refactored as such:
- Providers will hold the required parts of the schema in an internal `__required` variable
- By default, `Provider.required` will return that
- The `Provider.schema` method will merge that part when constructing the schema (This will require moving schema to an internal `__schema` class variable as well to retain consistency.

This change is potentially breaking for anyone that relies on the output of the `required` method

issue_title: DualBase.eval does not literalize it's terms, which results in a missing step of simplification.
issue_body:
For example:
```python
>>> parse("~A*~(~A+~B)")
AND(NOT(Symbol('A')), NOT(OR(NOT(Symbol('A')), NOT(Symbol('B')))))
```
By adding:
```python
term  = term.literalize()
```
after
```python
term = self.__class__(*args, eval=False)
```
to the method DualBase.eval, this fixes the problem:
```python
>>> parse("~A*~(~A+~B)")
FALSE
```

I have not reviewed the code for DualBase.eval to check that this will not cause any problems.

issue_title: Use oauth?
issue_body:
Hello @stianaske!
Let me introduce myself, I am Roberto Ostinelli, Neato Robotics' Director of Cloud Services.

I'm impressed with the work that you've put into integrating with our robots! Great job!

Given the interest that we've seen from developers like yourself and around the forums we've listened and have just released the [Neato Developer Network](https://developers.neatorobotics.com). In there, amongst other things you will find official documentation and SDKs for JavaScript, iOS and Android (for now, we plan on expanding to other languages too!).

This issue is for you to consider switching to proper OAuth instead of the internal logins mechanisms that you are using in this gem.

It would be the official way to proceed, and you would avoid some improper implementations. For example, you should not pass `token` and `platform` [here](https://github.com/kangguru/botvac/blob/v0.1.2/lib/botvac/cli.rb#L16): they are not needed, raise errors on our systems - since tokens are invalid - and this could eventually result in the blocking of user accounts by our automated systems if abused.

All the best,
r.

PS: I've opened the same issue on the [gem](https://github.com/kangguru/botvac) that, to my knowledge, started it all. :)

issue_title: BACpyes application for BBMD + Router
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm looking for a BACpypes stack that acts as both Router and BBMD. The Router could be any IP-to-X [1] Router where the IP side of the Router should also acts as a BBMD i.e. both Router and BBMD applications should bind to the same IP address.

I have referred the sample programs for BBMD [2] and IP2IPRouter [3]. They work fine when running separately binding to two different IP address from the same subnet. But I couldn't find any samples that highlights BBMD and BIPSimple in one stack. Are they meant to co-exist and bind to same IP address?

If we make UDPMultiplexer sharable [4] between BIPBBMD and BIPSimple applications, then we could make both of them bind to the same IP address. But the downside is all the broadcast between BIPBBMD and BIPSimple will be dropped by the UDPMultiplexer [5]. So broadcast communication from BBMD will not reach BIP and vice versa.

```
ip_address = "192.168.1.10/24:47808"

bip = BIPSimple()
bip_annexj = AnnexJCodec()

bbmd = BIPBBMD(ip_address)
bbmd_annexj = AnnexJCodec()

mux = UDPMultiplexer(ip_address)

bind(bip, bip_annexj, mux.annexJ)
bind(bbmd, bbmd_annexj, mix.annexJ)
```

I'm looking for suggestions on how to make an application that has both BIPBBMD and BIPSimple binding to same IP address. 

Thank you,

Sangeeth



[1] X could be IP, MSTP, Ethernet, etc.
[2] https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/blob/master/samples/BBMD.py
[3] https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/blob/master/samples/IP2IPRouter.py
[4] We could make reuse=True in UDPDirector - https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/blob/master/py27/bacpypes/bvllservice.py#L96 and that will allow to re-use the socket. 
[4-1] Also we could make UDPMultiplexer's annexJ (server) to deal with more than one serverPeer. Say, we can support a list of clients to bind to. In this case serverPeer could be [bip_annexj, bbmd_annexj]
[5] https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/blob/master/py27/bacpypes/bvllservice.py#L144-L147

issue_title: Sizing in generate_from_frequencies
issue_body:
This is incredibly helpful, thank you. Below: Some bug notes, some suggestions. Sorry to lump them all together.

I'm using `generate_from_frequencies` to lay out word clouds with relatively few words (~15). It seems like the input variable `frequencies` actually ignores the `count` in the tuple (I can't quite parse the purpose of `min(font_size, int(100 * np.log(count + 100)))`; seems like it should be relative to the canvas and/or use a normalized count?). I did modify the function to force a reduction in font size each time; otherwise it tended to chunk them-- a few one size, a few a little smaller, etc. I think this is due to the small number of words? issue_title: Fix HTTPS cache problems on pages with certificate errors
issue_body:
This is an optional fix. By default Chromium disables caching when there is certificate error. This patch will fix the HTTPS caching only when ApplicationSettings "ignore_certificate_errors" is set to True.

See http://www.magpcss.org/ceforum/viewtopic.php?f=6&t=11609

The patch suggested in the linked topic works fine after making some minor 
changes.

```
Index: net/http/http_cache_transaction.cc
===================================================================
--- http_cache_transaction.cc   (revision 241641)
+++ http_cache_transaction.cc   (working copy)
@@ -2240,7 +2240,8 @@
   // reverse-map the cert status to a net error and replay the net error.
   if ((cache_->mode() != RECORD &&
        response_.headers->HasHeaderValue("cache-control", "no-store")) ||
-      net::IsCertStatusError(response_.ssl_info.cert_status)) {
+       (!cache_->GetSession()->params().ignore_certificate_errors &&
+       net::IsCertStatusError(response_.ssl_info.cert_status))) {
     DoneWritingToEntry(false);
     ReportCacheActionFinish();
     if (net_log_.IsLoggingAllEvents())
```

The patch was verified to work with CEF 3 branch 1650 revision 1646. The https 
web server was nginx running on Ubuntu.

Patch instructions [available here](https://github.com/cztomczak/cefpython/wiki/BuildOnLinux#fix-https-caching-on-sites-with-ssl-certificate-errors-optional) (both BuildOnLinux and BuildOnWindows wiki pages).

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `czarek.t...@gmail.com` on 27 May 2014 at 8:38

issue_title: add --trusted mode to allow "npm install"
issue_body: running `npm install` installs any dependencies the repo specifies, which could include potentially dangerous dependencies that try to execute code as root. by default we shouldn't allow it.

issue_title: Software filtering of messages
issue_body:
Some interfaces support multiple filters, some support only one filter, and some do not support filtering at all or uses ranges instead of ID and mask (PCAN).

It could reduce the burden of the application if python-can filtered out messages according to the configured filtering in software. I [did this in the kvaser interface](https://github.com/hardbyte/python-can/blob/1.5.2/can/interfaces/kvaser/canlib.py#L448) before but I think it's better to implement something that all interfaces can use.

Suggestion is to implement this in either the BusABC class or in the utils package so that specific interface implementations can take advantage of them.

issue_title: Doesn't comply with Pocoo guidelines for approved extensions
issue_body:
Hi there.

This Issue is related to #76, but now it's about guidelines conformance and possible corner-cases, while the former was about the API usage (and initial request there has been fully implemented), so I think it's better to create a new one.

Flask extension development guidelines state that extension's `__init__` should set `self.app`, but `init_app` must not.
You can find it both [online](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/extensiondev/#the-extension-code) and in [copy of guidelines in this repo](https://github.com/lingthio/Flask-User/blob/ed65b106/misc/approved_extension.txt#L20-L21).

So Flask guidelines basically states that extension object should be a clean factory, and if there is some state depending both on extension and app object, `init_app` should register some new object (`app.extensions['ext_name'] = state_obj`).

Approved extensions do it this way:
[Flask-SQLAlchemy](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/flask-sqlalchemy/blob/ac135c67/flask_sqlalchemy/__init__.py#L804)
[Flask-DebugToolbar](https://github.com/mgood/flask-debugtoolbar/blob/14cd912d/flask_debugtoolbar/__init__.py#L74-L84)
[Flask-Mail](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-mail/blob/e195fca6/flask_mail.py#L581-L585)

I don't fully comprehend this design, yet guidelines state that it is the way to support multiple app instances running in the same process, which is important for scenarios like tests etc..
I also wasn't aware of this when creating #76.

How do you think - is refactoring required to comply with this guideline is feasible and important enough?

Is there interest to include these variations in an "Industry" class? Am open to changing the name! :)

issue_title: SIGINT got exception
issue_body:
after `honcho start`, press Ctrl-C, will yield exception:
```
...
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/honcho/command.py", line 220, in command_start
    manager.loop()

    msg = self.events.get(timeout=0.1)

    if timeout < 0 or not self._poll(timeout):
IOError: [Errno 4] Interrupted system call
```
this error could be simplely ignored IMO:
```diff
*** 111,116 ****
--- 111,120 ----
              except Empty:
                  if exit:
                      break
+             except IOError as e:
+                 if e.errno != errno.EINTR:
+                     raise
+                 exit = True
              else:
                  if msg.type == 'line':
                      self._printer.write(msg)
```
or use a alternative paradigm:
```diff
@@ -2,6 +2,7 @@ import datetime
 import multiprocessing
 import signal
 import sys
+import time

 from .colour import get_colours
 from .compat import Empty
@@ -107,10 +108,11 @@ class Manager(object):

         while 1:
             try:
-                msg = self.events.get(timeout=0.1)
+                msg = self.events.get(False)
             except Empty:
                 if exit:
                     break
+                time.sleep(0.1)
             else:
                 if msg.type == 'line':
                     self._printer.write(msg)
```

issue_title: using rest framework's IsAuthenticated break the condition evaluation in django 1.10
issue_body:
If you use `rest_framework.permissions.IsAuthenticated` the reduce operator will crash [here](https://github.com/caxap/rest_condition/blob/master/rest_condition/permissions.py#L97) because Django now returns a `CallableBool`.

This could be fixed by adding a callable test just after getting the result.

            if hasattr(result, '__call__'):
                result = result()

issue_title: review of incoming changes
issue_body:
# Pyhton version 
all

# Platform information
all

# _asammdf_ version
3.0.0dev

# Description

There are some topics regarding the upcoming 3.0.0 release that I would like to get some feed-back before releasing the code, so please share your opinion

1. mdf versions 2 and 3 are similar enough that the classes _MDF2_ and _MDF3_ have been merged in the new _MDF23_ class found in the file _mdf_v2_v3.py_. I couldn't come with a better naming and it feels a bit odd. Maybe we can come up with a better name

2. _MDF_ objects are now iterable so you can write code like this:
    ```python
    mdf = MDF(filename)
    for signal in mdf:
        # do something
    ```
    There are two things to decide here:

    1. at the moment the iterator is configurable to yield _Signal_ objects or _pandas DataFrame_:
        ```python
        from asammdf import MDF, configure
        configure(iter_channels=True)
        mdf = MDF(filename)
        for signal in mdf:
            # do something with Signal objects
        configure(iter_channels=False)
        for dataframe in mdf:
            # do something with the whole channel group as pandas DataFrame
        ```
        Should we keep this option or stick to yielding only _Signal_ objects?
    2. when the iterator is configured to yield _Signal_ objects the master channels are not yielded. 
        Should we also yield the master channel?

issue_title: `bitarray` does not register with `collections.Sequence`
issue_body:
Hi, I have been using `bitarray` recently, and I realized that `bitarray` objects are not registered with the `collections.Sequence` abstract base class, even though it implements the necessary `__len__` and `__getattr__`.

This makes code that checks for implementation of the `Container`, `Iterable`, `Sized`, and `Sequence` abstract base classes in the `collections` module fail when using `bitarray`.

issue_title: Nginx rewrite path wont appear on the djangular url
Wheels will break this.
issue_body: I notice enum-compat evaluates a conditional inside setup.py, which happens only at package build time. Thus, any wheels built with this—including ones built implicitly by pip for the local egg cache—won't have the ability to choose at runtime. You could probably get this working by using an environment marker instead. Cheers!

issue_title: modify service instance versioning strategy at registry
issue_body:
registry should provide an instance of the exact same version if available, else provide the next highest non breaking version

- should not broadcast new version availability as that will cause a denial of service because all clients will upgrade immediately, let the changes propagate naturally

remember to relax packet matching to comply with this logic at service-host and service-client

Reading the error message carefully, I found `pip` uses universal wheel `PyFunctional-0.7.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl`. Telling pip not to use wheel seems OK but it is inconvenient when you want to do `pip install -r requirements.txt` if you want to use wheel for the rest of the packages.

issue_title: Custom XML entities are coming out as None, as of 0.11
issue_body:
I use xmltodict to parse a rather complex XML file, the "JMdict" Japanese dictionary. The file defines a large number of custom entities, as shorthand for categories such as parts of speech.

I was previously using xmltodict 0.10.2 to parse the file, and it worked correctly. But now, the entities are coming out with values of None.

To reproduce:

- Download JMdict from http://ftp.monash.edu.au/pub/nihongo/JMdict.gz
- Extract the file and rename its extracted content to "JMdict.xml"
- Run this Python code (I'm running it in an IPython 5.1 shell on Python 3.5.3):

```python
>>> import xmltodict
>>> data = open('JMdict.xml').read()
>>> xml = xmltodict.parse(data)
>>> entry = xml['JMdict']['entry'][0]
>>> entry

OrderedDict([('ent_seq', '1000000'),
             ('r_ele',
              [OrderedDict([('reb', 'ヽ')]), OrderedDict([('reb', 'くりかえし')])]),
             ('sense',
              OrderedDict([('pos', None),
                           ('gloss',
                            [OrderedDict([('@xml:lang', 'eng'),
                                          ('#text',
                                           'repetition mark in katakana')]),
                             OrderedDict([('@xml:lang', 'ita'),
                                          ('#text',
                                           'simbolo di ripetizione in katakana')])])]))])
```

The entry being parsed looks like this:

```xml
<entry>
<ent_seq>1000000</ent_seq>
<r_ele>
<reb>ヽ</reb>
</r_ele>
<r_ele>
<reb>くりかえし</reb>
</r_ele>
<sense>
<pos>&n;</pos>
<gloss>repetition mark in katakana</gloss>
<gloss xml:lang="ger">(n) Wiederholung</gloss>
<gloss xml:lang="ger">Refrain</gloss>
</sense>
</entry>
```

Note that the value `&n;` came out as `None`. It used to come out as the string "noun (common) (futsuumeishi)", because line 306 contains:

```xml
<!ENTITY n "noun (common) (futsuumeishi)">
```

I'm working around this for now by downgrading to 0.10.2.

issue_title: The given sed pattern may not be enough to remove @@
issue_body:
Hi,

An MT system can generate hypotheses **ending** with a subword token which are not cleaned by the given sed pattern `s/\@\@ //g`:
```
echo 'foo@@ bar blah blah tot@@' | sed 's/@@ //g'
foobar blah blah tot@@

# Below seems to fix this by handling 0 or more spaces at the end
echo 'foo@@ bar blah blah tot@@' | sed 's/@@ *//g'
foobar blah blah tot
echo '
```

issue_title: Old filter stopped working recently
issue_body:
Dear Sergio,

Thank you for your great tool. I have been using a filter that creates a list of acronyms for almost two years, recent update caused it to stop working. Last time I used it was around five months back.

The filter is:


```
"""
Panflute filter that allows for acronyms in latex using the acro package

Usage:

- In markdown, use it as links: [SO](acro "Stack Overflow")
- Then, this filter will add \DeclareAcronym{LRU}{long=Least Recently Used} 
for the definition of LRU and finally \ac{LRU} to every time the term is used in the text.

(Development of this tool can be followed here https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pandoc-discuss/Bz1cG55BKjM)
"""

from string import Template  # using .format() is hard because of {} in tex
import panflute as pf

TEMPLATE_GLS = Template(r"\ac{$acronym}")
TEMPLATE_NEWACRONYM = Template(r"\DeclareAcronym{$acronym}{long=$definition}")


def prepare(doc):
    doc.acronyms = {}


def action(e, doc):
    if isinstance(e, pf.Link) and e.url == 'acro':
        acronym = pf.stringify(e)
        definition = e.title
        # Only update dictionary if definition is not empty
        if definition:
            doc.acronyms[acronym] = definition
        
        if doc.format == 'latex':
            tex = '\ac{{}}'.format(acronym)
            tex = TEMPLATE_GLS.safe_substitute(acronym=acronym)
            return pf.RawInline(tex, format='latex')


def finalize(doc):
    if doc.format == 'latex':
        tex = [r'\usepackage[]{acro}']
        for acronym, definition in doc.acronyms.items():
            tex_acronym = TEMPLATE_NEWACRONYM.safe_substitute(acronym=acronym, definition=definition)
            tex.append(tex_acronym)

        tex = [pf.MetaInlines(pf.RawInline(line, format='latex')) for line in tex]
        tex = pf.MetaList(*tex)
        if 'header-includes' in doc.metadata:
            doc.metadata['header-includes'].content.extend(tex)
        else:
            doc.metadata['header-includes'] = tex


def main(doc=None):
    return pf.run_filter(action, prepare=prepare, finalize=finalize, doc=doc) 


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

It is called with `-F acronyms.py`, but recently (as of yesterday) I have been welcomed with the error:

```

issue_title: Incorrect cache file path on Windows
issue_body:
When running Flask-Resize under windows, the generated cache path is invalid as it uses the OS libraries to generate the file path.

Eg when using `RESIZE_URL='http://127.0.0.1:5000'` and `RESIZE_ROOT='./static/images/'` results in the cache path `http://127.0.0.1:5000\static/images/cache/<hashed filename>`

The backslah after the `RESIZE_URL` is invalid as a web address.

issue_title: basePath gets ignored on route matching (bravado spec resources)
issue_body:
version 2.0.0-rc1, used as a tween alongside pyramid_tm and debugtoolbar

validation will try to match the full route.path against only the path defined in the swagger-paths object.

I.e. in the example:

      "basePath": "/api",
      "paths": {"/pets": {"get": {} } }

I.e. in the loop in Baravado spec (https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/spec.py#L114

It will not be able to match:

     pattern = (get, '/api/pets)

to

     resource = (get, '/pets')

issue_title: No need to pad HEX
issue_body:
Why do you pad the conversion with zeros?
There is no need to do this. 
`binary_array = '{:0>{width}b}'.format(int(hexstr, 16), width = hash_size * hash_size)`
to get rid of 0b like:
`bin(int('3838b59d0c1c7fd8', 16))`

just do
`'{:b}'.format(int('3838b59d0c1c7fd8', 16), width = hash_size * hash_size)`

or
format((int('3838b59d0c1c7fd8', 16)),'08b')

issue_title: Non-deterministic behavior with Python 3.3
issue_body:
When running the tests with Python 3.3.2 I get:

```
[gwidion@localhost multi_key_dict]$ python3 multi_key_dict.py 
All test passed OK!
[gwidion@localhost multi_key_dict]$ python3 multi_key_dict.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "multi_key_dict.py", line 621, in <module>
    test_multi_key_dict()
  File "multi_key_dict.py", line 464, in test_multi_key_dict
    assert (set(res) == items_for_str), 'items(str): expected {0}, but collected {1}'.format(items_for_str, res)
AssertionError: items(str): expected {('aa', '4'), ('something else', 'abcd')}, but collected {('mmm', '4'), ('something else', 'abcd')}
[gwidion@localhost multi_key_dict]$ python3 multi_key_dict.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "multi_key_dict.py", line 621, in <module>
    test_multi_key_dict()
  File "multi_key_dict.py", line 464, in test_multi_key_dict
    assert (set(res) == items_for_str), 'items(str): expected {0}, but collected {1}'.format(items_for_str, res)
AssertionError: items(str): expected {('something else', 'abcd'), ('aa', '4')}, but collected {('mmm', '4'), ('something else', 'abcd')}

All test passed OK!

So - this tests breaks about 50% the time. Please note that is not the test that requires fixing - it is exposing that different contents are available on the dict at different runs - it is likely there is code that relies on dictionaries yielding items always in the same order - in the same program run - that is not the case in modern Python (not even the latest 2.7)

issue_title: About 'get_available_name' signature
issue_body:
Hello all,

As I was investigating how to avoid S3 Boto storage to reuse files with the same name (and keep default Django's name generation policy) I found the `file_overwite` parameter in the storage and imagined that was a good clue to solve this situation.

Setting that to `False` and running my tests I received a `TypeError` at `S3BotoStorage`'s `get_available_name` function that indicated a superclass call with 3 arguments instead of the 2 required.

Here's the end of the traceback:
```
[...]
File "/Users/buddles/projects/perestroika-site/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/storages/backends/s3boto.py" in get_available_name
  522.         return super(S3BotoStorage, self).get_available_name(name, max_length)

Exception Type: TypeError at /aulas-online/1/arquivos/
Exception Value: get_available_name() takes exactly 2 arguments (3 given)
```

By looking at the source code, I found that this function has an argument called `max_length` that is not used in the function at all and is only passed to the superclass `Storage` that does not use it too (hence the exception).

So my question is:
- Is there something wrong with the function or that argument was left over and could be removed?

Thanks!

issue_title: Using get_user() in the UserDatastore instead of find_user()
issue_body:
## Issue:
I am extending the login/register routine and customizing the UserDatastore using username in addition to email (although this question would apply more to those not using an email) with custom Groups and other Items.  When automating role creation via signals (user_registered or user_confirmed) and passing the user to [add_role_to_user(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L99) it passes to [_prepare_role_modify_args(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L70) which in turn uses the [find_user](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L72) method which looks up only for the email for that user and returns none if something else is passed to it.  In my case I was passing the username, which causes it to find none. 

As mentioned I am extending the current UserDatastore and using similar methods for adding Groups and Projects/Items which have similar relationships to the User Model and thus using similar methods to do so.  I am using the [get_user()](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L87) method to capture my User Model which looks for the [indentity](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/utils.py#L377) as set in [configuration variable](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/core.py#L92) which returns the user model the same as the find_user() method.

## Question: 
Is there an advantage to using find_user() over get_user() in the role lookup?  It would seem using the get_user() method you would make it a bit easier to customize and would solve some of the issues with custom implementations that I have seen here and provide for more flexibility for the developer and extensibility in the future.

If get_user() was used instead of find_user() would it break something somewhere else or cause some compatibility issues that I am not seeing?  It would appear that get_user() is extended to the Peewee and MongoDB Classes and appear to return exactly the same as the SQL Class. 

This caused me a little bit of a headache figuring out, however I am able to use it as is with my current setup.  If you were not using an email in your model it would not be possible to use this method to add a user to a role and would require you to bypass large portions of the script to make your customization work.


I am happy make the changes and submit a PR if there are no objections.

issue_title: [RFC] marshal_with - filtering out non-required fields?
issue_body:
Hi all,
I've just started trying out this package (and I really like it - kudos!), but there are two things that I stumble upon when using `marshal_with` decorator.

**I wonder if there is a way to do the following**:
1. Filter out any non-required fields if they are missing in the marshal input data (instead of showing null values).
2. Raise `MarshallingError` (or any other) when required field is missing in the response data.

**Rationale and use cases**:

Ad 1: Sometimes there's a lot of optional response fields in the API and it simply makes the reponse unnecessarily big (and subjectively ugly) by displaying "nulls".
Ad 2: It might happen that once upon a time, due to some issue, external source data missing fields, etc - the end user might get the response and the issue will be completely silent.

Both of those cases are handled very well by i.e. Django Rest Framework serializers. It skips non-existing fields (as long as they are not required) and it raises an exception otherwise. I think it's a pretty intuitive and common pattern. Besides, the `required` parameter of the model fields is a bit misleading in this case as it does not do anything while serializing the response (and i.e. I use those models mainly to serialize the responses...)

Anyway, would you consider accepting small PR for the
**potential solution**?:
1. Introduce `check_required` and `skip_missing` parameters to `marshal_with` and `marshal_list_with` decorators.
2. Skip non-required fields when they are missing in the data when `skip_missing` is true.
3. Raise exception when required field is missing and when `check_required` is true.

Not to break any backwards compatibility the defaults would be `check_required=False` and `skip_missing=False`.

It looks to me that implementing call to some fields filtering function right after applying the masks here https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/marshalling.py#L50 would be relatively straightforward and convenient. Unfortunately, I don't know this package very well yet, so there might be better ways to go (without deep refactoring).

Thanks for your thoughts and the feedback.

issue_title: Can admin:index be overloaded in 3rd party templates?
issue_body:
While looking at this code I found out that after django1.7 the secret name for the login location was being leaked in the dummy page. After investigating I saw this line in honeypot_html:

Django administration

This was as a result of what was added to django1.7 in the base_sites.html file located here: .tox/django17_py34/lib/python3.4/site-packages/django/contrib/admin/templates/admin/base_site.html has the line:

{{ site_header|default:_('Django administration') }}

which contains the url admin:index. This is where the secret name is leaking. Once admin_honeypot is installed, anyone that uses admin:index in a template will resolve to the secret location and not the dummy location. I know for the app admin_honeypot this can be easily overloaded for it templates (though not easily the same for all version of django from 1.4 through 1.9). However even if admin_honeypot is installed if another app is using admin:index in its url the secret location will be leaking and an intruder can figure out to correct location. It is a big ask I think, but is it possible to somehow that when this app is installed, any where admin:index is encountered in any app under that django install be overloaded with the admin_honeypot:index url instead? With the exception of the real admin pages, hosted under the secret name.

issue_title: Doesn't work for `RelatedField` and subclasses.
issue_body:
The `isinstance(..., list)` check fails on queryset/manager values and so the `repr` of the queryset/manager object is displayed as a single object instead of showing a list of objects.

The advice is probably to just use `RelatedField`, but the reason for wanting to use `ListField` is to allow older API clients which are expecting to set and get a single value to do so without making any changes.

issue_title: Directives Firing Twice
undocumented feature: defining a DjangoObjectType as baseType for DjangoListObjectType or DjangoSerializerType
issue_body:
Hi,
first of all: awesome package!
I don't really have an issue to report, but a feature that should be documented and was hard for me to find.
My problem was that I intended to use DjangoSerializerType for common queries and mutations. Everything worked fine, apart from including property functions from the django model. I had to define a baseType like this, which worked fine:

```
class InstrumentType(DjangoObjectType):
    is_at = graphene.Field(LocationType.object_type(), source='is_at')
    class Meta:
        model = Instrument
```

But then I couldn't use the nice features of DjangoSerializerType or DjangoListObjectType. Then I searched for a long time how to pass that InstrumentType to e.g. a DjangoSerializerType. Finally I found out that you use this line in the types.py to get an already defined baseType from the registry:
```
output_type = registry.get_type_for_model(model)
if not output_type:
     output_type = factory_type('output', DjangoObjectType, **factory_kwargs)
```

But this depends on the order of the type definitions in the source code and is real implicit behaviour.
After I found that out, I could use my InstrumentSerializerType with correct custom baseType just like this:

```
class InstrumentSerializerType(DjangoSerializerType):
    class Meta:
        serializer_class = InstrumentSerializer
```

I really suggest you document that behaviour, since it's a powerful feature and can also have unexpected negative effects if you don't know about. 

Thanks for the nice work,
Greets,
Bene

issue_title: URL shortener for GitHub, Bitbucket, and Gitlab repo issues and commits
issue_body:
This would probably just get added to MagicLinks and parse links (probably as a treeprocessor).  What it would do is convert issues, and commits to shortened forms.  It would crawl links and find situations where the visible HTML matches the href.  And then it would see if the link matches one of the below forms and convert accordingly.  Notice Bitbucket and GitHub use 7 digits for hash, while Gitlab uses 8 (don't know why, but we would want to be consistent with what they use).  You would provide the base URL for your repo `https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions`.

Issues:

```
// Github
https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1
[#1](https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1)

// Bitbucket
https://bitbucket.org/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1/optional-title
[#1](https://bitbucket.org/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1/optional-title)

//Gitlab
https://gitlab.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1
[#1](https://gitlab.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/issues/1)
```

Commits:

```
// Github
https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98
[`d365e30`](https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98)

// Bitbucket
https://bitbucket.org/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commits/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98
[`d365e30`](https://bitbucket.org/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commits/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98)

//Gitlab
https://gitlab.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98
[`d365e30d`](https://gitlab.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/commit/d365e30dac0a50d5e2dcd950befc145f6a596e98)
```

Issues outside of your repo would be converted like in the form:

```
[username/repo#1](issue-url)
[username/repo@`hash`](commit-url)
```

If you provide no base url, the "outside of repo" form would be assumed.

issue_title: Add new rule for <comprehension>[0]
issue_body:
Suggest change from `<comprehension>[0]` `next(<generator>)`, e.g.

```diff
-[x for x in y if x == z][0]
+next(x for x in y if x == z)
```

This may be a bad idea as there would be the change of exception from `IndexError` to `StopIteration` if there are no elements returned by the generator. But it could also be possible to do the following to avoid the overhead of list creation:

```diff
-try:
-    v = [x for x in y if x == z][0]
-except IndexError:
-    v = 123
+v = next((x for x in y if x == z), 123)
```
This may not be feasible as it would be quite complicated to implement sanely.

issue_title: Logging Issues
issue_body:
Essentially a continuation of issue #1, opening a separate issue as the old one is closed, get the message `No handlers could be found for logger "apscheduler.executors.default"`. This happens when a error is present in the scheduler code. (The function that is called by the scheduler).  
To test, please run this. It's modified off the one in the examples folder to throw an error.

https://gist.github.com/HackToHell/b8c73a2c6db53e9881ac
Output 
```
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
 * Restarting with stat
No handlers could be found for logger "apscheduler.executors.default"
No handlers could be found for logger "apscheduler.executors.default"
```
It seems to be an issue with APScheduler library itself, https://bitbucket.org/agronholm/apscheduler/issues/78/no-handlers-could-be-found-for-logger. So the suggested method to catch the errors is to have a basic logging setup ( http://stackoverflow.com/q/28724459/787563 ). I tried that and added it to the code. This resulted in flasks logging system's output being killed off. 
I added 
``` python
import logging
logging.basicConfig()
``` 
after the imports.

New Output - Shows the proper output.

[This](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28724459/no-handlers-could-be-found-for-logger-apscheduler-executors-default#comment45817724_28724459) comment in the same SO thread says the very same to author of the library, however he says [that it should not be the case](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28724459/no-handlers-could-be-found-for-logger-apscheduler-executors-default#comment45846108_28724459). issue_title: Many Application Instances: What should I expect?
issue_body:
I'm still trying to wrap my head around a lot of Python's and Flasks' deeper workings but some initial prototyping with APScheduler has me concerned about about how scheduled tasks will play out in a distributed application environment.

The first encounter was with Flask's debug/reload functionality triggering the scheduler twice. I handled that with based on this solution:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9449101/how-to-stop-flask-from-initialising-twice-in-debug-mode

But now I'm coming up on things that are harder to test in my development environment. This isn't meant as a substitute for that level of testing, but more so to get an idea what I *should* expect to see from it. I need a baseline.

First is Gunicorn. The application needs to be multithreaded to serve a high volume of requests without them queuing up as they would with the Flask development server. Gunicorn is one of the standards and it will fork the Flask application multiple times. In this case 8.

Then, for high availability, we have dual staging/production hosts that are load balanced. That means the application running 8 times on two different hosts into the same MySQL database, which is being used as the JobStore.

All of our jobs are defined in our configuration file and that can be tailored per host, but not per fork. The jobs will either be cron-based or interval-based with unique IDs, 1 max instance, and 'replace_existing' as True.

I **think** we'll be safe with those settings given the last initiated instance would simply replace any existing jobs of the same unique ID, preventing duplication, but I'm not sure if there's any other foreseeable effects of multiple schedulers working off the same configuration and same jobstore.

Any guidance would be appreciated.

issue_title: JSFiles object is not reversible
issue_body:
In DJango 2.0, `JSFiles` object needs to be reversible, otherwise it raises an error

> TypeError at /some_url
> 'JSFiles' object is not reversible

The issue can be solved by either renaming the `__iter__` method to `__reversed__` or by adding a `__reversed__` method to `JSFiles` Object. Adding a method instead of renaming keeps backward compatibility.

issue_title: Cannot create customer with password
issue_body:
The following code will fail with an error `{u'password_confirmation': [u"doesn't match Password"]}`:

```python
import shopify

c = shopify.Customer()
c.email = 'foo@bar.com'
c.password = 'foo'
c.password_confirmation = 'foo'
c.save()
```

`c.__dict__` is:

```python
{'errors': <pyactiveresource.activeresource.Errors object at 0x431df10>, '_initialized': True, '_prefix_options': {}, 'klass': <class 'shopify.resources.customer.Customer'>, 'attributes': {'password_confirmation': 'foo', 'email': u'foo@bar.com'}, 'password': 'foo'}
```
As you can see the `password` attribute is not in the `attributes`. A workaround is to set the password in the `__init__`:

```python
c = shopify.Customer(attributes={'password': 'foo', 'password_confirmation': 'foo'})
```

issue_title: Support for searching using created_at_range and updated_at_range parameters
issue_body:
Two parameters, created_at_range and updated_at_range, listed in the [Box api docs](https://developer.box.com/v2.0/reference) (see Searching for Content),  are currently not supported.

According to the docs: 
#### created_at_range

> The date when the item was created. Specify the date range using RFC3339 timestamps separated by a comma. For example: `2014-05-15T13:35:01-07:00,2014-05-17T13:35:01-07:00. Either the beginning date or the ending date may be empty, but the separating comma is required. For example, if you omit the beginning date, then the ending date must begin with a comma.

#### updated_at_range
> The date when the item was last updated. Specify the date range using RFC3339 timestamps separated by a comma. For example: `2014-05-15T13:35:01-07:00,2014-05-17T13:35:01-07:00. Either the beginning date or the ending date may be empty, but the separating comma is required. For example, if you omit the beginning date, then the ending date must begin with a comma.

I figured I would create a feature request to get the discussion started about how to implement this feature.

The way the current search interface has been implemented suggests that adding two kwargs, created_at_range and updated_at_range, to the existing `Client.search` method, would make the most sense.

```
class Client(Cloneable):
    ...
    @api_call
    def search(
            self,
            query,
            limit,
            offset,
            ancestor_folders=None,
            file_extensions=None,
            metadata_filters=None,
            result_type=None,
            content_types=None,
            updated_at_range=None,
            created_at_range=None
    ):
        ...
```

There is the question of type, however, which I feel should be discussed. Should the newly added parameters be strings or a container type? If a container type (list or tuple), should its members be strings or date-times? Should a class be created to encapsulate the range? 

One could argue that the date-time string format (rfc3339) is an implementation detail that should be handled by this library, not by the user, and thus supplying a container of `datetime` objects would be preferable. However, since either the start or end of the range may be null, specifying an argument of the form `updated_at_range=(None, datetime.now())`, for example, might not be preferable.

What are peoples thoughts on this? I've forked and implemented a version which implemented the tuple-of-datetimes approach (as in `updated_at_range=(start_dt, end_dt)`) and would be happy to issue PR or refactor it, depending on the outcome of the conversion here.

issue_title: Documentation for MultiBoxPrior is not substantial
issue_body:
The documentation for [`mxnet.ndarray.contrib.MultiBoxPrior`](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/ndarray/contrib.html?highlight=multibo#mxnet.ndarray.contrib.MultiBoxPrior) is as follows:

`mxnet.ndarray.contrib.MultiBoxPrior(data=None, sizes=_Null, ratios=_Null, clip=_Null, steps=_Null, offsets=_Null, out=None, name=None, **kwargs)`

_Null is not a valid default parameter.
Searching the git repo, I could not find a python api for MultiBoxPrior (to modify it for my own needs)

although the c version can be found here https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/contrib/multibox_prior.cc

This holds true for all the other SSD box detection.

If a python level function is available I would like to know where it is. If not, I would like enough documentation to understand the c code.

issue_title: Slicing mx.nd.array with postive start and neg end alone same axis
issue_body:
Current mx.nd.array does not support slicing with positive start end negative end alone same axis while numpy can do it.
```python
import os
import sys
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx

A = np.asarray([i for i in range(16)])
A = A.reshape((4,4))
B = mx.nd.array(A)
print A[1:-1] #ok
print B[1,:-1] #ok
print B[1:-1] #wrong
A[1:-1] = 0
print A #ok
B[1:-1] = 0
print B #wrong
```

I'm writing a new `mx.operator.CustomOp` and in the `forward()` method, the engine hangs (no CPU activity) when converting my numpy array to an NDArray: `mxy = mx.nd.array(y)`.

I have MXNET_CPU_WORKER_NTHREADS=2, as the example and docs say, which smells like there's some kind of race condition at play.  And in fact if I turn this off, then the sample code in example/numpy-ops/custom_softmax.py fails in the same operation.

So I'm guessing I'm hitting whatever bug can sometimes be avoided by setting the using multiple worker threads, but in my case that's not a sufficient workaround.  What else can I try?

issue_labels: [{'id': 206159502, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDYxNTk1MDI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Bug', 'name': 'Bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': False}, {'id': 206159505, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDYxNTk1MDU=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Call%20for%20Contribution', 'name': 'Call for Contribution', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 718413113, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3MTg0MTMxMTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Example', 'name': 'Example', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 775221378, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzUyMjEzNzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/RNN', 'name': 'RNN', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 337768593, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMzc3Njg1OTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Scala', 'name': 'Scala', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This model has not been updated since 2017/07, the RNN example for LstmBucketing is no longer runnable. 
Please contribute to RNN support on Scala for the current version. If you would like to run this example for now, please revert your MXNet version to `v0.12`. 

In summary, I suspect that some operator changes caused this issue.

- [ ] LstmBucketing
- [ ] TestCharRnn
- [x] TrainCharRNN

Please contact @lanking520 or @nswamy if you would like to dig in and fix this problem. The following is a way to reproduce the issues I found:

## LSTM Bucketing
### Setup
Download the required ptb file from here:
```Bash
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dmlc/web-data/master/mxnet/ptb/ptb.train.txt
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dmlc/web-data/master/mxnet/ptb/ptb.valid.txt
```
You can use this [script](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/scala-package/examples/scripts/rnn/run_lstm_bucketing.sh) to run the model 
or manually identify the file path by following [this](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/scala-package/examples/src/main/scala/org/apache/mxnetexamples/rnn/LstmBucketing.scala#L36-L48)

#### Additional step to get it work

issue_title: custom loss function
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm fine tuning Resnet following the https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/faq/finetune.html tutorial.

Tutorial modifies the Resnet like that:
```
    all_layers = symbol.get_internals()
    net = all_layers[layer_name+'_output']
    net = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=net, num_hidden=num_classes, name='fc1')
    net = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=net, name='softmax')
    new_args = dict({k:arg_params[k] for k in arg_params if 'fc1' not in k})
    return (net, new_args)
```

However, I need to create a different kind of loss function. Which is following : 
1) Do softmax(just softmax not softmaxoutput) with 100 classes.
2) Compute dot product between softmax probabilities and  the vector from [1, 2, 3 ... 100]. (This vector should not change during the training)
3)Use LinearRegressionOutput

        net = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=net, num_hidden=num_classes, name='fc1')
        net = mx.symbol.softmax(data=net, name='softmax_intermediate')
        net = mx.symbol.dot(lhs=net, rhs= mx.nd.array(range(1,101)), name = "givi")

issue_title: (info.type) != (kNotInitialized)
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm hitting a bug, for which I can't create a small repro. I have a rather complex model with several LSTMs and attentions mixed up together, and as part of the model I have a `Concat` operation. When I run the model, I hit an error on line 650 in graph_executor.cc: `(info.type) != (kNotInitialized)`. The op that fails is a `Concat` of four inputs, all of shape (1, 200), along the 2-st axis (one-based), with inputs coming from `Embedding` or `FullyConnected` ops, or element wise multiplication.

I added some tracing info:

```
CHECK_NE(info.type, kNotInitialized) << graph_.nodes[nid].name << " i=" << i << " nid=" << nid << " ord=" << ord << " total=" << graph_.nodes[nid].inputs.size() << " shape=" << info.shape << " prev node=" << graph_.nodes[topo_order_[i - 1]].name;
```

(ord is an extra counter that counts iterations of the `for (StaticGraph::DataEntry e : graph_.nodes[nid].inputs) {`)

And this is what I observe:

```
(info.type) != (kNotInitialized) attn_single_concat_backward i=912 nid=910 ord=2 total=4 shape=(1,200) prev node=fullyconnected106_backward
```

Here's where it gets interesting:
1. Note that the `ord=2`, which means that the third input to the Concat op is not initialized. If I swap the first and the third inputs to the `Concat` and rerun it, it still fails on `ord=2`. So it seems like there's nothing wrong with the second input per se, since replacing it with previously working first input still makes it fail on the same ordinal.
2. If I rearrange inputs to the Concat further, sometimes `total` becomes equal to three (in other words, a Concat op that has `num_args=4` somehow only has three inputs?)

I understand that this is not enough information to debug it. So I have some questions that would help me get more info:
1. Inputs to the `*_backward` operations are the inputs to the corresponding symbol, right? If yes, what can explain the fact that I see 3 reported inputs to a Concat op that has `num_args=4`?
2. What is the meaning of `kNotInitialized`? The input to the op is an output of another op. Does it suggest that the corresponding output is somehow unreachable?

issue_title: Hole when activate file not written
issue_body:
An untimely ^C can create a partially created virtualenv (this simulates that, internally there's at least one report of this happening):

```
$ make virtualenv_run
	./bin/venv-update \
		venv= virtualenv_run/ --python=python3.5 \
		install= -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt \
		bootstrap-deps= -r requirements-bootstrap.txt \
		pip-command= pymonkey pip-custom-platform -- pip-faster install --upgrade --prune
> virtualenv virtualenv_run/ --python=python3.5
.: 1: Can't open virtualenv_run/bin/activate

Something went wrong! Sending 'virtualenv_run/' back in time, so make knows it's invalid.
make: *** [virtualenv_run] Error 2
$ make virtualenv_run
	./bin/venv-update \
		venv= virtualenv_run/ --python=python3.5 \
		install= -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt \
		bootstrap-deps= -r requirements-bootstrap.txt \
		pip-command= pymonkey pip-custom-platform -- pip-faster install --upgrade --prune
> virtualenv virtualenv_run/ --python=python3.5
.: 1: Can't open virtualenv_run/bin/activate

Something went wrong! Sending 'virtualenv_run/' back in time, so make knows it's invalid.
make: *** [virtualenv_run] Error 2
```

One can simulate this by:

```
make virtualenv_run
rm virtualenv_run/bin/activate
touch requirements.txt
make virtualenv_run
```

issue_title: Pickadate has no visual indication when input data is incomplete and would not be submitted
issue_body:
See also: https://github.com/plone/Products.CMFPlone/issues/271
The expires field uses a Pickadate pattern. Only selecting a date and saving will result in the date being silently discarded. This is because the widget data is only updated, if both time and date is set.
If one only sets a time value, it is hard to decide. what a good default value might be for date.
So maybe the pickadate widget should have a visual indication if data is incomplete and would not be saved.

issue_title: More food for thought
issue_body:
So there seems to be something wrong with the test runner, or the tests make the test runner do weird things...

If I run (on buildout.coredev 5.0 latest commit as I write)
```
./bin/buildout -c jenkins.cfg
./bin/jenkins-test -s plone.app.discussion
```

The output looks like:
```
Running plone.app.discussion.testing.PloneAppDiscussion:Functional tests:
...
Running plone.app.discussion.testing.PloneAppDiscussion:Integration tests:
...
```
So two layers, both from p.a.discussion itself run.

If instead I run the same but for P.CMFDiffTool we get the following:
```
Running Products.CMFDiffTool.testing.Products.CMFDiffTool.AT:functional tests:
...
Running Products.CMFDiffTool.testing.Products.CMFDiffTool.DX:functional tests:
...
Running Products.CMFDiffTool.testing.Products.CMFDiffTool:functional tests:
...
Running Testing.ZopeTestCase.layer.ZopeLite tests:
...
Running plone.app.contenttypes.testing.PloneAppContenttypes:Functional tests:
...
Running plone.app.testing.layers.Plone:Integration tests:
...
Running zope.testrunner.layer.UnitTests tests:
...
```
So not only 3 different CMFDiffTool functional layers, but also a ZopeTestCase layer, a p.a.contenttypes layer, p.a.testing layer and a zope.testrunner layer too.

Playing around I discovered that removing  ``testFieldDiff.py``, ``testListDiff.py`` and ``testTextDiff.py`` vanishes the ZopeTestCase layer in the output. The same happens with ``testChangeSet.py`` and the p.a.contenttypes layer.

Still no idea which tests bring the p.a.testing and zope.testrunner layers...

Is that an isolation problem? a bug in the test runner?

cc @tisto @tomgross @jensens
