issue_title: Angular distance in Readme
issue_body: I realize that Annoy uses Euclidean distance of normalized vectors for its angular distance, which for two vectors u,v is equal to 2(1-cos(u,v)). The readme currently mentions cosine similarity but not how it is connected to the angular distance that is actually calculated. It would be nice to have this reflected somewhere in the readme as values greater than 1 in the distance could confuse users who expect the angular 'cosine distance' to be just 1-cos(u,v). I for example spent some time checking for mistakes as I was wondering if I had to normalize the vectors myself before adding them as I was unaware that the definition of angular distance was 2(1-cos(u,v)) and expected values in [0,1].

issue_title: Make new area check in FindDegenerates optional
issue_body:
I just pulled latest and noticed that one of our models now fails to convert because of the new area check when trying to find degenerates. It removes all triangles from a mesh which in turn causes assimp to throw an exception.

My thoughts on this: The threshold for the area is 1e-6. That by itself might be a little too aggressive, especially when using meters as metric convention. Second, the area is calculated without taking the node transform into consideration. So the actual size being calculated is not taking any scaling into account.

Therefore, it would be great if the area check could be enabled or disabled with a new post process flag.

issue_title: Computing the hazard statistics appears to be ultra-slow
issue_body:
In truth, the computation of the hazard curves is ultra-fast and for instance in the recent SHARE calculation by @mmpagani (https://oq1.wilson.openquake.org/engine/18150/outputs) it takes only 86 seconds per core. However, there is a big data transfer issue: 341.74 GB of PoEs are being transferred via rabbitmq/celery and this takes  33,169 seconds, i.e. over 9 hours. The data transfer totally dominates the computation time. This is a known problem that has a workaround, i.e. run the statistics in postprocessing.
However users never do that because it is inconvenient and counter-intuitive. The solution is to change the engine in such a way to work in post-processing mode even if we are not in post-processing. Thanks to the recent change in https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/3710 this is now possible, it is enough to store the PoEs in the new .hdf5 file introduced there and read that file from the workers, thus avoiding the celery/rabbitmq data transfer.

issue_title: Looking for pointers in speeding up boto3 initialization
issue_body:
I am using boto3 in the context of AWS Lambda, which bills us for each slice of 100ms elapsed processing a script.

The biggest share of my processing time is spent initializing boto3 runtime clients and resources. For instance, by batching 3 items, I noticed that the first item took 7 seconds to complete, then it took less than 1 second to complete the rest of the items. This makes individual requests very costly. Occasionally, AWS seems to re-use the runtime context for different requests when items are coming in fast enough. On these occasions, I also noticed a total runtime of less than 500ms average even though the handler function received a single item.

Even though AWS Lambda provides the boto3 package, I wouldn't mind packaging a homebrewed, "static" version of the package with my code if that can save me a few seconds. But I'm not sure where to start, or if this is even possible given boto3's dynamic model.

python bibclassify_cli.py -s -n 10 -k HEPontCore.rdf  title_abstract_keywords.txt

issue_title: RegEx validator matches only from the beginning of the string
issue_body:
The current version of Colander’s `RegEx()` validator calls Python’s [`match()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.match) function, see source [here](https://github.com/Pylons/colander/blob/5e4d2c6fa4f6dc86465516ee93f45be61539c7e4/colander/__init__.py#L345). That function matches only the beginning of the string, not the entire given string.

This should be documented as it impacts the regular expressions used.

As of Python 3.4, [`fullmatch()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.fullmatch) should be considered to replace the call to `match()`.

issue_title: Repo.blame don't return multi lines.
issue_body:
According to API reference,
 -------------------------
 The blame information for the given file at the given revision.
 
 Parm rev:	revision specifier, see git-rev-parse for viable options.
 Returns:	list: [git.Commit, list: [<line>]] A list of tuples associating a Commit object with a list of lines that changed within the given commit. The Commit objects will be given in order of appearance.
 -------------------------
blame method returns list of lines but I think the method always returns only one line.
The method pick up the first line of lines which were written in same commit (other lines are skipped and method don't return its information).

The behavior come from the lien 620 of git/repo/base.py.
The variable 'info' is initialized only when a line is first line of same commit.
But 'info' will be None after processing a line (the lien is not commit information).

We have to initialize 'info' to process subsequent lines.

issue_title: Possibly misleading info in pypi; python3 compatibility
issue_body:
Metadata on pypi of comtypes lists python 3 as one of the languages.
(see https://pypi.org/project/comtypes/)

But comtypes-1.1.4 is not compatible with python 3.
(see pypi.org/project/comtypes/#files ; comtypes-1.1.4.zip\comtypes\__init__.py:1026. It uses type `unicode`, which is not available in python 3. I have checked python 3.4 and later, but I'm sure it is missing also from previous python3.x versions)

issue_title: Input waypoints
issue_body:
The directions, distance and surface APIs all require *input waypoints*. Let's specify this in more detail...

At the python SDK level, all of the services take an iterable/list of GeoJSON-like feature mappings. At the command line, it's not clear to me exactly what the inputs will be.

The CLI will take `x` and normalize it to a list of features where `x` could potentially be

- line-delimited GeoJSON features on the stdin stream (output of fio cat)
- path to a GeoJSON file containing a featurecollection of points (probably the most common case?)
- paths to GeoJSON files each containing point features (makes interop with geocoding easier since you're likely to get one feature per call)
- stream or file of GeoJSON geometries
- stream or multiple args with JSON arrays of coordinate pairs `"[lat, lon]"` (ala the input to reverse geocoder)
- ... others? ...

So the question is, **What types of inputs will we support for waypoints?** On one hand I want to support the most common workflows even if that means accepting a bunch of different input formats. On the other hand, unix design principles - let other tools do the lifting to get data into our expected format.

And on a related note, if we're accepting GeoJSON and the service requires points, how should we handle other geometry types? Automatically explode multipoints and lines to points? Skip them? Be strict? Most of these validation decisions will likely be implemented at the SDK level but it might be useful to discuss them in this context.

issue_title: Custom serialization of non-finite (inf/nan) floats
issue_body:
It would be great to be able to handle serialization of non JSON serializable floats (the non-finites: ``inf``, ``-inf``, ``nan``). Preferably, in a manner consistent with the existing mechanisms for serialization of non-JSON types -- the user-supplied ``default`` function.

Something like:

    >>> simplejson.dumps(float('inf'), allow_nan=False, default=lambda x: {"$repr": repr(x)})
    '{"$repr": "inf"}'

If you think this would be worthwhile, I'm willing to work on a pull request (I submitted a first draft Python-only implementation, let me know if you agree with the direction).

Btw, this issue is tangential to #149, which can be addressed similarly.

issue_title: Update to Phoenix 5.7.1.0
issue_body:
https://github.com/CrossTheRoadElec/Phoenix-frc-lib/releases/tag/Phoenix_v5.7.1.0

One major issue: there is now a `CCIEXPORT` macro used to add a visibility attribute to every CCI function prototype. It doesn't play so well with CppHeaderParser (although we might be fine with assuming what the return types should be). A workaround could be to replace all occurrences of `CCIEXPORT` with the empty string, but I don't think that's ideal.

issue_title: Why no loops in IterativeRobot?
issue_body:
As an experienced programmer, I don't undertsand why you shouldn't use loops in `xxxPeriodic`.

> Avoid using loops, as unexpected conditions may cause you to lose control of your robot.

So no terminating for or while loops? 

> as unexpected conditions may cause you to lose control of your robot.

That's just a risk you have to take. Loops are a key aspect of programming. With enough testing, this can be prevented. And a timer can be used to interrupt the loop if it goes for too long.

issue_title: magicbot: add default_state decorator
issue_body: A state that will execute if nothing else is executing. Technically, this is almost the same as overriding execute... but I think it's more intuitive to have a separate mechanism for it.

issue_title: Pipfile.lock versions don't match Pipfile
issue_body:
I have an odd issue, and I'm not sure if I'm doing something wrong since I just starting using pipenv, but something seems off with the sub-dependency checking. 

With a brand new project, if I do `pipenv install channels==1.1.8`, I get the following output:

```
Successfully installed Django-2.0.3 asgiref-1.1.2 channels-1.1.8 daphne-1.4.2
```

That is what I would expect. But the lock file has version `asgiref==2.2.0`, and `daphne==2.1.0`. Which are obviously different versions than pip actually installed.

If I then then try to run `pipenv install asgiref==1.1.2`, I get the dependency resolution error:

```
Warning: Your dependencies could not be resolved. You likely have a mismatch in your sub-dependencies.
  You can use $ pipenv install --skip-lock to bypass this mechanism, then run $ pipenv graph to inspect the situation.
Could not find a version that matches asgiref==1.1.2,~=2.1
```

If I run with verbose (`pipenv install asgiref==1.1.2 -v`), it tells me `channels 1.1.8` requires `asgiref~=2.1`:

```
channels==1.1.8           requires asgiref~=2.1, daphne~=2.0, Django>=1.11
```

issue_title: Question: Detecting conditional dependencies managed by python code and not markers
issue_body:
`pipenv` can create `Pipenv.lock` containing hashes for all available package formats. This allows the same package be safely installed in multiple execution environments such as Linux or Windows where compiled versions of C-based packages are likely to differ.

`Pipfile.lock` also contains markers, so if some of installed libraries used markers to declare conditional dependencies (e.g. only for python version 2.x), it will be present in `Pipfile.lock` and all things are great.

Problem: Some `setup.py` code I have seen is building list of dependencies `install_requires` dynamically using pure python code and not using markers.

Question: Is `pipenv` able to detect python code defined conditional list of dependencies (not using markers) and reflect it properly in `Pipfile.lock`?

I can imagine how complex such task could be and complete implementation is probably impossible.

If such scenarios exists (and I would assume they exist), it would be great to describe these limitations in documentation and also to recommend best practice to package authors to refrain from pure code created list of dependencies and use exclusively markers.

issue_title: Add switch, --ignore-all-dev-stuff-completely
issue_body:
I have a library** with small number of dependencies and a large number of common development dependencies ([see here](https://github.com/matthewdeanmartin/jiggle_version/blob/master/Pipfile)), like black or pylint. One solution would be to not use black or pylint since they their versioning and conflicting deps are a hot mess, but I don't like that idea.

Because I can't tell pipenv to ignore dev dependencies, pipenv upgrade fails, `pipenv lock` fails, `pipenv lock -r` fails. I generally can only run `pipenv * --no-lock`

So I created a copy of Pipfile, Pipfile_nodev, manually removed all dev dependencies and then ran this scritp and, now I have a freeze file!

This feels like too much work for a common scenario. Am I doing this wrong or is there a missing feature?

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
rm -rf /tmp/freezer
mkdir /tmp/freezer
mkdir /tmp/freezer/jiggle_version
cp -r ./jiggle_version/ /tmp/freezer/jiggle_version/
cp ./Pipfile_nodev /tmp/freezer/Pipfile
cd /tmp/freezer
pipenv lock
pipenv lock -r
rm -rf /tmp/freezer
```

I also asked this related Q on StackOverflow and got no response:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51273884/how-do-i-upgrade-a-pipenv-package-and-ignore-prerelease-packages

** Yes, I know libs aren't supposed to freeze requirements, only apps, but I don't have a handy public app to illustrate this.

issue_title: Support an option parameters which can install binary files also in last version
issue_body:
Dear Jason R,

We are failed to  install our libs to teams after upgrade the  setuptools to 20.2.2.  Some lib update is been stopped. And not find other good way to do the post handling.

We have to include some third party binary files during setup :(  as we cannot develop the python function to cover all requirement.

We are very eager for you to support an option parameters which can support install binary files also during setup.

Thanks!!

Best regards,
Harte.

issue_title: Setuptools' sdist doesn't use data_files while distutils does
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **ionelmc (Bitbucket: [ionelmc](http://bitbucket.org/ionelmc), GitHub: [ionelmc](http://github.com/ionelmc))**

----------------------------------------

Distutils has this: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/distutils/command/sdist.py#L270-L282

However that code is not copies in the sdist provided by setuptools. 

This can be problematic if you don't inlcude a file in MANIFEST.in but you have it in data_files - python's docs will most certainly mislead you as they say that sdist uses data_files: https://docs.python.org/3/distutils/sourcedist.html?highlight=data_files#specifying-the-files-to-distribute (last bullet)

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/issue/521

issue_title: Create the static maps component
issue_body:
Static maps will map any lib. The way we're mapping bower(when this component used to be called assets) could be used to map other applications/libs.

The static maps is another way to get static resouces mapped without changing the application or the component, the user will be able to map static content using the static_maps.yml/yaml file.

issue_title: BalancedConsumer.consume() should block/fail during rebalance or if zk connection is pending/dead
issue_body: This comes out of #206. Clearly there are use cases where, if the zookeeper connection goes away and so you can't know if you still own your partitions, you should not be consuming from them. That's probably the safest option, ie to block/fail completely, and is what's the core of this commit (which would need redoing because it depends on a lot of other commits on that branch): https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/3b23fadeacfec18b91d22aaf121a349a329d02cc

issue_title: Pykafka 2.4.1 release date
issue_body:
Is there any chance that we can get the current master released as pykafka version 2.4.1?

There are some very useful bugfixes in master for bugs that we're seeing production.  It's also been 7 weeks since 2.4.0 was released, so we're hoping it's about time for a version bump.

If it's not going to happen any time soon, would you mind giving us a rough date of when 2.4.1 will be released?

Thanks for all the great work you guys are doing on pykafka!

issue_title: Local run of Kafka-JVM
issue_body:
First of all, thanks for your work.

I try to run kafka-jvm with localhost.
All seems to be fine, I send messages from a NodeJS app which go through kafka topics to arrive at the Spout. 

Zookeeper connection work, kafka connection seems to work because messages arrive but I have two errors (I thinks linked):

1) First I have 
> Read partition information from: /consumers/fifi/partition_0  --> null
> No partition information found, using configuration to determine offset
when storm run with sparse running

2) Secondly, I have a more confusing error which appear only when the spout receive his first messages:
>INFO  org.apache.storm.zookeeper.server.PrepRequestProcessor - Got user-level KeeperException when processing sessionid:0x1518e430784000e type:create cxid:0x3 zxid:0x2a txntype:-1 reqpath:n/a Error Path:/rootPath/consumerID Error:KeeperErrorCode = NoNode for /rootPath/consumerID

At the end, I see with zookeepercli that the spout has never create the consumer in /consumers.


Do you have any idea why it's appear? Or at least some way to search... ? I actually search arround google without result.

issue_title: How to access multiple tuples in Bolt
issue_body:
My topology in short is as follows:

```python
from streamparse import Topology, Spout, Bolt, Stream


class Dataspout(Spout):
     outputs = [Stream(fields=['to_validate'], name='to_validate'),
                Stream(fields=['to_combine'],  name='to_combine')]

     def next_tuple(self):
          ###Does something
          self.emit(value_for_validate, stream='to_validate')
          self.emit(value_for_combine,  stream='to_combine')


class ValidateBolt(Bolt):
     outputs = [Stream(fields=['validated1', 'validated2'], name='validated')]

     def process(self, tup):
          ###Does something
          self.emit([a_list, b_list], stream='validated')     


class CombineBolt(Bolt):
     outputs = []
     
     def process(self, tup):
          """----------Highlight: My question is here-----------"""


class MyTopology(Topology):
     data_spout    = Dataspout.spec()
     validate_bolt = ValidateBolt.spec(inputs=[data_spout['to_validate']])
     combine_bolt  = CombineBolt.spec(inputs=[validate_bolt['validated'],
                                              data_spout['to_combine']]) 
```

Ideally, in the highlighted part I wish I could access the ```[a_list, b_list]``` emitted by ```validate_bolt``` and the ```value_for_combine``` emitted by ```data_spout``` in my ```combine_bolt```. However, in the ```process(self, tup)``` function of ```CombineBolt```, the ```tup.values``` only contains the tuple emitted by ```data_spout```. (When I print out ```tup.values```, it writes Data_Spoutto_validateTuple(to_validate=...) ) How could I access the tuple emitted by ```ValidationBolt```?

issue_title: Spouts ack/fail methods are never called
issue_body:
I was trying to ACK RabbitMQ messages after processing and found out that ack/fail spout's methods are never called. I needed this, because you can confirm message in RabbitMQ only from the same channel that send the message. Hence the ack method.

### How to reproduce:
1) use wordcount example

    sparse quickstart wordcount

2) modify WordSpout:

    class WordSpout(Spout):
    
        def initialize(self, stormconf, context):
            self.words = itertools.cycle(['dog', 'cat',
                                          'zebra', 'elephant'])
    
        def next_tuple(self):
            self.log("next tuple")
            word = next(self.words)
            self.emit([word])
    
        def ack(self, tup_id):
            self.log("acking")
    
        def fail(self, tup_id):
            self.log("failing")

3) modify WordCounter:

    class WordCounter(Bolt):
    
        auto_ack = False
        auto_fail = False
    
        def initialize(self, conf, ctx):
            self.counts = Counter()
    
        def process(self, tup):
            word = tup.values[0]
            self.counts[word] += 1
            self.emit([word, self.counts[word]])
            self.ack(tup)
            self.log('%s: %d' % (word, self.counts[word]))


You can use self.fail(tup) instead of self.ack()
There is no "acking" / "failing" output in log.

issue_title: LogCapture persists records between parent and child tests
issue_body:
When attempting to capture during a unittest that uses subclassing, the tests fail due to LogCapture maintaining records between two logically distinct test cases.  

```python
from unittest import TestCase
import logging
from testfixtures import log_capture

logger = logging.getLogger(__file__)

class FixtureTest(TestCase):
    @log_capture(level=logging.ERROR)
    def test_my_fixture(self, capture):
        log_single_error()
        self.assertEqual(len(capture.records), 1, capture.records)

class FixtureSubclassTest(TestFixtureSanity):
    pass
    # test_my_fixture is invoked when this TestCase is run through inheritance

def log_single_error():
    logger.error('my awesome error')
```

```
.F

issue_title: doctest fail in segmentation.rst
group stars from different iterations in the PSF Photometry algorithms
issue_body:
I think it would make sense to put stars which are identified in different iterations, but which overlap each other, in the same group. Further, one could re-fit that group with the correct model (i.e., correct number of stars), instead of treating stars identified in different iterations independently.

So, a pseudo code might be

```
1. perform psf photometry at iteration n
2. look for remaining stars in the residual image (iteration n+1)
3. put every star found in step 2 in the group that they overlap with.
4. using the data from iteration n, perform psf photometry with the new groups
5. repeat until either a given number of iterations is reached or no stars are found
```

issue_title: Table format when a list of apertures is provided
issue_body:
It is handy to be able to do photometry with multiple aperture radii with one call, but not so handy that the output is recorded in separate columns, and the radii aren't recorded in the output.

For my typical workflow I would prefer to have the radius as a column name and have the photometry all in a single column. Then I can just filter the output on the aperture radius (and that radius is recorded in the row with the position and the flux, which I think is generally good practice for reproducability).

issue_title: Document HEALPix projection with Astropy
issue_body:
I remember that when first encountering HEALPix I was very confused, because it's a pixelisation and a WCS projection, and I couldn't find docs how to work with the HEALPix projection in Astropy.

Specifically, this doesn't apply the projection, and doesn't give an error, it just passes the values through without change:
```python
>>> from astropy.wcs import WCS
>>> wcs = WCS(key='HPX')
>>> print(wcs)
WCS Keywords

Number of WCS axes: 2
CTYPE : ''  ''  
CRVAL : 0.0  0.0  
CRPIX : 0.0  0.0  
PC1_1 PC1_2  : 1.0  0.0  
PC2_1 PC2_2  : 0.0  1.0  
CDELT : 1.0  1.0  
NAXIS : 0  0
>>> print(wcs.wcs_world2pix(42, 43, 1))
[array(42.), array(43.)]
>>> print(wcs.wcs_pix2world(42, 43, 1))
[array(42.), array(43.)]
```

I did manage to access the projection via the [astropy.modeling.projections.Sky2Pix_HEALPix](http://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.modeling.projections.Sky2Pix_HEALPix.html) wrapper for that WCS transform:
```python
>>> from astropy.coordinates import Angle
>>> from astropy.modeling.models import Sky2Pix_HEALPix, Pix2Sky_HEALPix
>>> x, y = Sky2Pix_HEALPix()(-148, 65)
>>> print(x, y)
-141.89216598555333 66.14250235769997
>>> print(Angle(x, 'deg').rad, Angle(y, 'deg').rad)
-2.4764854792342104 1.1544044416499766
>>> lon, lat = Pix2Sky_HEALPix()(x, y)
>>> print(lon, lat)
-147.99999999999997 65.0
```

This is the example from [here](https://github.com/substack/healpix#example) and the results are identical.

I have two questions:
1. How can I use `astropy.wcs.WCS` to achieve the HEALPix projection?
2. Why does the HEALPix projection yield values in the range `X = {0, 360}` and `Y={-90, 90}`, i.e. values in the same range as `LON = {0, 360}` and `LAT={-90, 90}`, making it easy to think `(X, Y)` would be angles again (which they aren't)?

For the second question, I think it's a bit of a convention to have `X=LON`, another scale factor, e.g. `X=LON/360` could have been chosen? And for `Y`, is there a fundamental reason why it has the same range as `LAT`, or is is a bit by chance that it comes out to `Y={-90, 90}`?

Once these two points are clarified, I'd like to make a small docs addition in `astropy-healpix` to clarify this, and give an example how one can do the HEALPix projection with Astropy (since it's not exposed in the astropy-healpix API).

issue_title: skimage.measure.label connectivity=None produces strange results
issue_body:
The function skimage.measure.label produces strange results when connectivity=None.

According to the documentation connectivity=None should be equivalent to '3' when working with a 3D-image. However, results are different to setting connectivity=3. While the latter produces correct results, connectivity=None produces very strange and incorrect results. For some completely NON-connected components the same label is given while some labels contain not a single voxel (= are empty). However, the total number of labels is the same for connectivity=3 and connectivity=None.

issue_title: Installation fails for amazonlinux Docker image on 2015 Macbook Pro
issue_body:
### What did you do?
`pip install pillow-simd`

### What did you expect to happen?
I am probably in over my skis here, but any help would be appreciated.

> The installation itself is simple just as running `$ pip install pillow-simd`, and if you're using SSE4-capable CPU everything should run smoothly.

For what it is worth, on a separate build, `pip install pillow` succeeds.

issue_title: TP: Experiment with a different way to prevent oversampling from bursting columns
issue_body: Currently, we have a separate inference state and learn state, and learn state only allows one predicted cell per column. Instead, it would be cleaner to combine them and modify `chooseCellsToLearnFrom` to sample at most one cell per column. Experiment with this approach, and see how it affects performance.

issue_title: Swarming - implement crossvalidation for the data
issue_body:
If I didn't miss something in http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KuFfm3ncEwI and https://github.com/numenta/nupic/wiki/Running-Swarms on running swarms, there's a huge "mistake" in handling the data - training and then testing on the same dataset is pointless, as it leads to overfitting (and the doc does not mention need to split, sorry if it's considered so obvious that it was skipped). 

Either way, it'd be nice if swarming as a `tuning framework` provided a tool to do the split to `training/validation/test` data. 

There are 2 most common ways to do it: 
* a simple 60%/20%/10% split. 
* or better, `k-fold crossvalidation` 

I'd like something like: 

`swarming --split-dataset=crossvalidation --folds=10 --orig-data=mydata.csv` 

and will produce: test.csv (with say 10% of randomly select data) 

and from the rest: 
  train_0.csv (90% from "the rest")
  validation_0.csv (10% from the rest) 
  etc...

issue_title: Allow predictions for multiple fields per model
issue_body:
It would be nice if you could specify multiple fields to make predictions for.

This will require a change to the CLAModel to support multiple classifier regions. It currently supports only one with the region name "Classifier":
https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/0cc904e5835a45e541eb0cac92901271b8f91108/nupic/frameworks/opf/clamodel.py#L1117

Additionally, the OPF and description.py format will need to be updated to support multiple fields to predict. It require some changes to metrics or swarming so it knows which field (or fields?) to use for optimization.

There are two implementations of the classifier, so we will need a task in nupic.core for the C++ implementation.

issue_title: Move audiostream example out of nupic
issue_body:
> from https://discourse.numenta.org/t/proposal-move-audiostream-example-out-of-nupic-codebase/1251

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/tree/master/examples/audiostream is a community-contributed example codebase, but because it is inside the NuPIC repository, it implies that maintainers of the repo will keep it running and up-to-date, yet this is not the case. I don't think we have tried to run this code in years. It might not even work anymore.

Anyway, since we aren't maintaining this code, I think we should move it out of NuPIC. I propose we move this code into https://github.com/htm-community/nupic-example-code, where I think more community examples of NuPIC usage should go.


- [ ] push code into https://github.com/htm-community/nupic-example-code
- [ ] delete code from nupic

issue_title: Package nupic.core source code in extensions/core when building distributions
issue_body: In order to fall back to local compilation of `nupic.core` in the event of an internet outage or missing binary file, we need to include the entire C++ code of `nupic.core` inside the nupic package that gets published to pypi or S3 or wherever.

issue_title: Batch response status and transaction rollback
issue_body:
Currently the `POST /batch` response status is:
* 200 if everything goes smooth
* 200 if a sub-response returns a 4XX status as expected by protocol (e.g. 400, 401, 403, 404, 412)
* 50X if a sub-response crashes (e.g 500, 503)

Current the transaction of the whole batch is:
* committed if everything goes smooth
* committed if a sub-response returns a 4XX status expected by protocol
* rolled back if a sub-response crashes

With the current implementation (based on `pyramid_tm` tween) the transaction final state depends of the final batch response status [(>=200 <400)](https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid_tm/blob/master/pyramid_tm/__init__.py#L28).
It is very easy to change.

But, we have to decide whether we want to:

1. return 200 and commit transaction when a sub-response is 4XX (*ie. keep current behaviour*)
1. return 4XX and rollback transaction when a sub-response is 4XX
1. return 200 and rollback transaction when a sub-response is 4XX

Thoughts ?

> Notes:
> * delete can generate 404 response if record was already deleted
> * https://github.com/mozilla-services/syncto/issues/78

issue_title: Date comparison fails due to incompatible datetimes
issue_body:
If you define an `after date` condition [as described in the documentation](https://github.com/cfpb/wagtail-flags#after-date):

```py
# settings.py
FLAGS = {
   'condition': {'after date': '2018-02-01T00:00'}
}
```

The code that tries to make the comparison fails due to this exception:

```py
>>> timezone.now() > dateparse.parse_datetime('2018-02-01T00:00')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes
```

This is because Django's `timezone.now()` returns an `datetime` that is aware of its timezone, but parsing a string like `'2018-02-01T00:00'` doesn't provide the same information.

One simple solution would be to require (and modify documentation to state) that the timestamp string passed in have some offset information, e.g. `'2018-02-01T00:00Z'` or `'2018-02-01T00:00-04:00'`:

```py
>>> timezone.now() > dateparse.parse_datetime('2018-02-01T00:00Z')
True
```

@willbarton how would you feel about this change? Is there some way to enforce this? It feels suboptimal that an error due to misformatted timestamp is hidden and results in a failed check, without any way for the user to know that things are configured wrong.

issue_title: BibConvert: do not interpret "EOL" keywords in source field value
issue_body:
Originally on 2011-04-12

When some source field contains string "EOL", BibConvert replaces **EOL** with a new line (\n). For eg. for a source field:

```
$ cat MYFIELD
The School was jointly organised and sponsored by CERN, INFN, ACEOLE (a Marie Curie Initial Training Network at CERN)
```


with the following basic template:

```
MYFIELD---<datafield tag="520" ind1="" ind2=""><subfield code="a"><:MYFIELD::MYFIELD:></subfield></datafield>
```

would result in:

```
<datafield tag="520" ind1="" ind2=""><subfield code="a">The School was jointly organised and sponsored by CERN, INFN, AC
E (a Marie Curie Initial Training Network at CERN)</subfield></datafield>
```

This is probably not wanted. It should be possible to disable this behaviour, or/and it should be documented in the BibConvert admin guide.

(Other similar, but less intrusive substitutions are performed by BibConvert:
```
def sub_keywd(out):
    "bibconvert keywords literal substitution"

    out = string.replace(out, "EOL", "\n")
    out = string.replace(out, "_CR_", "\r")
    out = string.replace(out, "_LF_", "\n")
    out = string.replace(out, "\\", '\\')
    out = string.replace(out, "\r", '\r')
    out = string.replace(out, "BSLASH", '\\')
    out = string.replace(out, "COMMA", ',')
    out = string.replace(out, "LEFTB", '[')
    out = string.replace(out, "RIGHTB", ']')
    out = string.replace(out, "LEFTP", '(')
    out = string.replace(out, "RIGHTP", ')')

    return out
```
)

issue_title: ModelClass as class variable for the Record class
issue_body:
It would be great to have the ModelClass i.g. `RecordMetadata` as class variable to be able to push different type of records in different tables by derivate the `Record` class such as:

```python
class Record(RecordBase):
    """Define API for metadata creation and manipulation."""
    clsmodel = RecordMetadata

    @classmethod
    def create(cls, data, id_=None, **kwargs):
        with db.session.begin_nested():
            record = cls(data)
            before_record_insert.send(
                current_app._get_current_object(),
                record=record
            )
            record.validate(**kwargs)
            record.model = cls.clsmodel(id=id_, json=record)
            db.session.add(record.model)
        after_record_insert.send(
            current_app._get_current_object(),
            record=record
        )
        return record

class DerivatedRecord(Record):
    """Record in a different table."""
    clsmodel = DerivatedRecordMetadata
```

issue_title: Tool.getSortedImportSteps should return a deterministic order
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope-cmf/+bug/1418881, Dieter Maurer reported:

> Products.GenericSetup 1.7.3

> "GenericSetup" supports the declaration of step dependencies and "getSortedImportSteps" returns a sequence of steps respecting those declarations. However, the step order is otherwise non-deterministic. This makes it extremely hard to detect missing step dependency declarations.

> https://dev.plone.org/ticket/13815 shows an example where a missing step dependency was not recognized for a very long time.

> I have spent the last few days to track down another similar problem: I start Zope and get the following (unworking) sort order:

> 2015-02-05T08:50:19 INFO bfd.theme_bp.utilities.tracing Return Products.GenericSetup.tool.getSortedImportSteps -> (u'plone-difftool', u'plone.app.caching', u'properties', u'Products.Ploneboard.uninstallVarious', u'rolemap', u'sharing', u'ploneopenid-various', u'collective.z3cform.datetimewidget_various', u'toolset', u'reference_catalog', u'various', u'typeinfo', u'cmfeditions_various', u'repositorytool', u'kupu-setup', u'workflow', u'update-workflow-rolemap', u'placeful_workflow', u'placeful_marker', u'catalog', u'content', u'cookie_authentication', u'caching_policy_mgr', u'various-calendar', u'plone.app.theming', u'uid_catalog', u'componentregistry', u'portlets', u'contentrules', u'atcttool', u'factorytool', u'actions', u'plonetheme.sunburst-various', u'controlpanel', u'memberdata-properties', u'plonepas', u'mailhost', u'action-icons', u'solr', u'browserlayer', u'content_type_registry', u'mimetypes-registry-various', u'kss_mimetype', u'difftool', u'tinymce_various', u'plone.app.registry', u'tinymce_settings', u'plone-final', u'plone-content', u'skins', u'jsregistry', u'bfd.gui.various', u'cssregistry', u'jquerytools-various', u'kssregistry', u'propertiestool', u'viewlets', u'archetypes-various', u'archetypetool', u'plone_outputfilters_various'

> I restart Zope (without any modification) and get the following (working) sort order:

> 2015-02-05T08:55:18 INFO bfd.theme_bp.utilities.tracing Return Products.GenericSetup.tool.getSortedImportSteps -> (u'properties', u'rolemap', u'sharing', u'toolset', u'uid_catalog', u'various-calendar', u'plone.app.theming', u'various', u'componentregistry', u'plone_outputfilters_various', u'action-icons', u'memberdata-properties', u'skins', u'bfd.gui.various', u'kssregistry', u'cssregistry', u'jquerytools-various', u'jsregistry', u'tinymce_settings', u'tinymce_various', u'browserlayer', u'content_type_registry', u'mimetypes-registry-various', u'kss_mimetype', u'mailhost', u'propertiestool', u'actions', u'plonetheme.sunburst-various', u'controlpanel', u'plonepas', u'viewlets', u'difftool', u'plone.app.registry', u'solr', u'archetypes-various', u'archetypetool', u'catalog', u'atcttool', u'cookie_authentication', u'typeinfo', u'factorytool', u'kupu-setup', u'cmfeditions_various', u'repositorytool', u'reference_catalog', u'caching_policy_mgr', u'workflow', u'content', u'portlets', u'plone-final', u'plone-content', u'contentrules', u'placeful_workflow', u'placeful_marker', u'update-workflow-rolemap', u'ploneopenid-various', u'collective.z3cform.datetimewidget_various', u'Products.Ploneboard.uninstallVarious', u'plone-difftool', u'plone.app.caching', u'ploneboard-various')

> Of course, the real problem are missing dependency declarations for the Plone "plone-final" import step (it depends on "skins"). However, the non deterministic behaviour of "getSortedImportSteps" hides this problem quite efficiently.

issue_title: Pyjnius class mocking
issue_body:
Hello,

I am using pyjnius in a project. I am attempting of mocking classes generated by pyjnius, created through `autoclass` and `cast`. I'm using [pytest-mock](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-mock), which is just a wrapper of [unittest's mock](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html). At this point I'm stuck in a possible solution:

pytest-mock requires the fully qualified python class name that is to be mocked. However, as the python class (generated by pyjnius) is generated at runtime, pytest-mock cannot access it. I think that this problem is ortogonal to the mocking framework itself, as I can't explicitly reference the generated class either (through, for instance, `jnius.reflect.generated.MyClass()`). The usual error is:
`AttributeError: module 'jnius.reflect' has no attribute 'generated'`

Has anyone tried this before? Is there any documentation that I'm missing?

issue_title: NE3: Pickling of C-NumExpr object
issue_body:
Currently the C-NumExpr object cannot be pickled.  Pickling of the C-NumExpr object would permit serialization of the module.  This could be valuable in two cases: 1.) Pickling of the wisdom (formally cache) dictionary, which stores all the compiled programs.  This could speed loading of pre-optimized Python code by a wisdom bank functionality similar to that seen in FFTW.  2.) Serialization within the context of parallel processes, as within the `multiprocessing` module or similar.  This could potentially allow for parallel processing libraries (`dask`, etc.) to use NE3 in a hybrid multi-processing/multi-threading environment, similar to the way OpenMPI hybrid mode permits the same.  Thus NE3 could leverage the RAM conservation and memory sharing of threads, while multiple processes are spawned in distributed/cluster environments.

The main issue as I see it is how to accommodate the constants.  These are the only components outside of `NumExprProgram` and `NumExprRegister` that are created in `NumExpr_init`, as the temporaries are initialized in the interpreter itself.  Practically we should investigate if the constant arrays really need to be BLOCK_SIZE in shape.  I suspect if a singleton array is broadcast with a multi-dimensional NumPy array, that a stride of zero is returned. In Python an empty tuple is returned; it remains to be seen what the C-API returns.  In this case, the BLOCK_SIZE shaped const is really a waste of L1 cache space.

issue_title: We should use font-awesome's icons.yml rather than our generated charmap.json
issue_body:
Currently, qtawesome uses a character map that is stored as a .json file for the association 
(icon-name -> unicode character). I generated these files based using yml source available in FontAwesome and Elusive icons.

Font-awesome:   https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/blob/master/src/icons.yml
Elusive icons:  https://github.com/reduxframework/elusive-icons/blob/master/src/icons.yml

Fortunately, they use the same schema in both projects.

To adapt more easily to future versions of FontAwesome and Elusive icons, we should probably directly use those yaml files. However, this would require qtawesome to have a dependency to yml. Unlike json, yml is not part of the Python standard library.

Similarly, I would have to produce a similar file for the spyder font.

issue_title: Feature tortuosity in NeuroM
issue_body:
Tortuosity is defined as the ratio of the section path to the section radial distance and reflects the "curvature" of the neurite. It is an essential feature for the synthesis process and its validation, as well as the general evaluation of synthesised cells. I think it is useful to have it as a feature in order to avoid user errors.
The tortuosity can be computed since the features involved (section path and radial distances) already exist in NeuroM. But if and only if their values are in an one-to-one correspondence. Are they?

issue_title: Fontawesome Helper to manage icons
issue_body:
### Problem 

Fontawesome is amazing and it always makes me feel fascinated. However, massive quality icons bring great challenges to our memory. I often worry about forgetting the name of an "icon" or finding what I want. 

### Solution

So I wrote a icon assistant to solve this problem. It can help you find, manage and share your icons conveniently and quickly. Believe that its simple and generous interaction style can make you easy to get started.

Click here and visit it [Fontawesome helper](http://fontawesome.hukaihe.com/)

### Why would other Font Awesome users care about this?

I wanna share my strength to the fontawesome community but it is limited in both idea and energy. So I need to know how many people will benefit from it to decide to keep maintaining it or not, so tell me your idea about it.
PS: the next thing I am going to improve may be the multilingual service.
[FontawesomeHelper in github](https://github.com/HuKaihe/FontAwesomeHelper)

### Feature request checklist

- [x] This is a single feature
- [x] I have [searched for existing issues](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome-Pro/issues) and to the best of my knowledge this is not a duplicate

issue_title: Patroni not trying to reconnect to other etcd members
issue_body:
I'm seeing a bit of an odd case under test that I can't quite explain.  Wondering if you have any ideas.

This is with Patroni 1.3.6 with Python 2.7

We have a deployment with 2 Postgres Members and 3 etcd members deployed across three AZ's, lets call them AZ's X, Y, and Z

We have etcd's in each AZ, assume the name matches the AZ
etcdX
ectdY
etcdZ

and Postgres in two AZ's
pgX
pgY

All three etcd members are up and have formed a cluster by the time Patroni starts on both PG members.  We have a connection to etcd hard coded to connect to etcdX on startup as such

```
etcd:
  host: <etcdX_IP>:4001
```

pgX becomes the master, pgY becomes a follower.

To test the failure, we fence off  AZ X at the network layer from AZ's Y & Z. 

pgX times out communicating with etcdX (presumably due to lack of quorum reads).  It attempts to connect to etcdY and etcdZ and fails as expected.  pgX goes read-only as expected.

pgY loses connectivity with etcdX.  It does not try and reconnect to etcdY or etcdZ.  It  simply keeps retrying to connect to etcdX.

I'd expect that pgY should also be trying to reconnect to one of the other etcd members, but it does not.  Any idea why?

We do plan on upgrading to the latest version and giving it the entire list of etcd hosts on startup, but I'm still interested in digging into this failure more.

issue_title: Could not load library ".../libpqwalreceiver.so"
issue_body:
We compile and install Postgres in a non-standard location (/opt/custom/share/postgresql-9.6.2_7 in the example below) which requires that we specify the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` env var when starting up postgres and when working with postgres utilities. When starting up Postgres, Patroni currently passes along `PATH`, `LC_ALL` and `LANG` env vars if they are set, but does not do the same with `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`. This results in the following FATAL replication error on the replica node:
```
2018-06-05 21:10:26,675 INFO: Lock owner: postgresdb-01; I am postgresdb-02
2018-06-05 21:10:26,675 INFO: does not have lock
2018-06-05 21:10:26,676 INFO: no action.  i am a secondary and i am following a leader
FATAL:  could not load library "/opt/custom/share/postgresql-9.6.2_7/lib/libpqwalreceiver.so": libpq.so.5: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
FATAL:  could not load library "/opt/custom/share/postgresql-9.6.2_7/lib/libpqwalreceiver.so": libpq.so.5: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

The fix for this is to pass `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` along with the other env vars, which results in replication behaving as expected:
```
2018-06-05 22:03:08,722 INFO: Lock owner: postgresdb-01; I am postgresdb-02
2018-06-05 22:03:08,722 INFO: does not have lock
2018-06-05 22:03:08,727 DEBUG: Sending request(xid=49): SetData(path='/service/postgres_cluster/members/postgresdb-02', data='{"conn_url":"postgres://postgresdb-02:5432/postgres","api_url":"http://postgresdb-02:8008/patroni","timeline":1,"state":"running","role":"replica","xlog_location":184664496}', version=-1)
2018-06-05 22:03:08,737 DEBUG: Received response(xid=49): ZnodeStat(czxid=4294967413, mzxid=4294967497, ctime=1528235848173, mtime=1528236188727, version=28, cversion=0, aversion=0, ephemeralOwner=216194533074731009, dataLength=169, numChildren=0, pzxid=4294967413)
2018-06-05 22:03:08,741 INFO: no action.  i am a secondary and i am following a leader
```

I will submit a pull request shortly that addresses this problem.

Différences entre Simulateur et Trace
issue_body:
Bonjour à tous, 

J'utilise simultanément l'outil Trace et l'outil Simulateur et il s'avère que la variable de retour "revdisp" n'a pas la même valeur dans les deux outils malgré des variables d'entrées identiques.

Exemple : Un indiviudu, salaire de 20 000, dividendes de 5 000 et assurancevie de plus de 8 ans avec PFL de 5000.
Revenu disponible retourné par le simulateur = 22 892
Revenu disponible retourné par Trace = 24 488

La différence se trouve dans le RSA + aides exceptionnelles de fin d'année + minimas sociaux qui sont en plus dans l'outil Trace. Ces prestations sociales me paraissent fausses car elles sont toujours présentes malgré un revenu élevé.

Trace est l'outil qui permet d'utiliser l'API et c'est donc la valeur qui va ressortir pour les utilisateurs, ce problème est donc majeur.

Merci de m'aider à trouver une solution 👍

issue_title: unicode strings sneak into html template and break interactive maps
Refinements in search_disjunctive_faceting override params
issue_body:
In [this method](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-client-python/blob/88d30f8f4f943976e5eb282e98200e76194ade64/algoliasearch/index.py#L419), the optional refinements param override the params. Meaning that if I want to add a facet filter, I have to put it in the refinements, which is not the case in all other APIs. 
What should logically happen is that the refinements should only be appended to the regular params.

issue_title: Deploy error when not granted lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration permission
issue_body:
Should it be possible to deploy Lambda functions without granting the `lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration` IAM permission? Our use case is that we would like to be able to:
- deploy Lambda functions and API endpoints via Chalice
- allow these functions to access resources in our private VPC, by associating them with specific subnets and security groups in the VPC (to access RDS)
- prevent the modification/creation of Lambda functions such that they become associated with non-Lambda designated subnets/security groups

AWS Support suggested the above could be achieved by disabling the `lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration` permission. issue_title: Make Bucket.delete() in storage more restricted / opinionated
issue_body:
UPDATED 1/26/14 by @dhermes

After discussion with @jgeewax the approach will be

- Cap at 512 object deletes (or similar number) and cowardly refuse if more objects exist
- Don't try to deal with retries or 409s, just raise the error and give info
- RE: eventual consistency, just ignore (maybe log?) 404s for object deletes

------------------

See @thobrla [comment][1]:

> ...If you provide this functionality, it must be made extremely clear that this is a
> non-atomic operation that is not expected to succeed if there are concurrent writers to
> the bucket. The danger here is providing the illusion of atomicity to the client, especially
> because that illusion is likely to work at small scale and then fail cryptically at large scale.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/535#issuecomment-71094336

issue_title: Emulator doesn't differentiate between MORE_RESULTS_AFTER_LIMIT and NO_MORE_RESULTS
issue_body:
This is a result of the emulator being built
on the Cloud Datastore API for App Engine, which only distinguishes between
NOT_FINISHED or not. Since the `more_results` flag is a hint (see below),
the only safe response when the query is finished is
MORE_RESULTS_AFTER_LIMIT.

Something to note:

`more_results` is a best effort hint to the developer. In the case of the
emulator it is buggy that `MORE_RESULTS_AFTER_LIMIT` is returned even if
the query didn't reach the limit. However even in production, the query may
return `MORE_RESULTS_AFTER_LIMIT` even if there are no more results if the
server is unwilling to do the work to check for the next entity.

This is not the issue here but for posterity: in order to determine if you
should keep iterating through results the clients should only ever use
`more_results == NOT_FINISHED a`a loop condition.

issue_title: Generic Exception "Please verify server_context is configured correctly."
issue_body:
If a database insert fails, we get a generic error > Please verify server_context is configured correctly.

Ex:

Doing an insert with a duplicate constraint results in:
[WARNING: 240 2018-01-30 15:19:08,152] cannot insert plate 3500001280 because 'Please verify server_context is configured correctly.'

Grabbing the e.__context__ shows the actual exception:

[WARNING: 334 2018-01-30 15:18:32,451] could not add plate 3500001280 because '400: ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "uq_plate_barcode"\n  Detail: Key (barcode)=(3500001280) already exists.'

I think this is because here:

https://github.com/LabKey/labkey-api-python/blob/2338b74e209d075868269d7bb322b92ff14b81ad/labkey/utils.py#L68

and here:

https://github.com/LabKey/labkey-api-python/blob/2338b74e209d075868269d7bb322b92ff14b81ad/labkey/exceptions.py#L72

the original exception is being swallowed.

replacing this:
return switcher.get(type(e), 'Please verify server_context is configured correctly.')

with:
return switcher.get(type(e), str(e) if str(e) else 'Please verify server_context is configured correctly.')

should allow the user to see the original exception.

issue_title: Getting simplified regulons list
issue_body:
I used the pyscenic command line interface (CLI) and it is a bit limited in that it won't return a simple list of pruned regulons with their targets. I believe this is important for interpreting the AUC matrix of activity scores and knowing what the targets are of the transcription factors. I cobbled together some python code to get this list for downstream analysis (see below). If you could make this standard output from the `pyscenic ctx' output that would be helpful. The current 'CTX' pruned file has a non-standard means of parsing and appears to have a lot of information that is of secondary interest. 

``` python
import pandas as pd
import os
from pyscenic.transform import df2regulons as df2regs
import json

# use function from pyscenic for parsing the 'pyscenic ctx' output
def _df2regulons(fname):
  ext = os.path.splitext(fname,)[1]
  df = pd.read_csv(fname, sep=',', index_col=[0,1], header=[0,1], skipinitialspace=True)
  df[('Enrichment', 'Context')] = df[('Enrichment', 'Context')].apply(lambda s: eval(s))
  df[('Enrichment', 'TargetGenes')] = df[('Enrichment', 'TargetGenes')].apply(lambda s: eval(s))
  return df2regs(df)

# Form dictionary of { TF : Target } pairs from 'pyscenic ctx' output.
rdict = {}
regulons = _df2regulons('joint_ctx.csv')
for reg in regulons:
  targets = [ target for target in reg.gene2weight ]
  rdict[reg.name] = targets

# Write to JSON for import to R
rjson = json.dumps(rdict)
f = open("regulons.json","w")
f.write(rjson)
f.close()
```

Then export into R with : 

```r
library(jsonlite)
regs <- read_json('regulons.json', simplify = T)
```

issue_title: ZigBee / Digimesh 16 vs 64 bit destination address default values?
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm using Series 1 XBees with Digimesh 2.4 firmware (VR=8073) and successfully using them via the `ZigBee` object (thanks for the recent documentation update! ;) )

There is one niggle I'm trying to work out though: the ZigBee object requires both a `dest_addr_long` (64 bit) and a `dest_addr` (16 bit) as there are no defaults provided. https://github.com/nioinnovation/python-xbee/blob/master/xbee/zigbee.py#L54

For my Digimesh xbees, the 16bit `dest_addr` seems to be ignored completely (which is in accordance with the digimesh Transmit Frame docs)

Cross referencing the ZigBee and Digimesh docs, it looks like the Digimesh protocol has marked the old 16bit address field as **RESERVED Set to 0xFFFE**

The ZigBee spec has "Set to the 16-bit address of the destination device, if known. Set
to 0xFFFE if the address is unknown, or if sending a broadcast."

Would there be any issues with setting the default for the 16 bit addr to 0xFFFE? That would save Digmesh users having to explicitly set it to an arbitrary value. Not sure if it has any negative implications for ZigBee users though?
I notice the ZigBee `remote_at` command has defaults set for both addresses: https://github.com/nioinnovation/python-xbee/blob/master/xbee/zigbee.py#L45

Alternatively, having a `tx_long_addr` available for ZigBee/Digimesh, like at https://github.com/nioinnovation/python-xbee/blob/master/xbee/ieee.py#L52 would help avoid having to set the 16bit addr.

I'm happy to raise a PR, but wanted to check whether a default 16bit addr of 0xFFFE makes sense. I imagine ZIgBee users might get frustrated having to enter both addresses too, should there be a default 64bit address also?

Thanks!

issue_title: Codalab workers deployment on Grid Computing qsub
issue_body:
Could you please share more details of how the codalab workers were modified to work on systems like computing grids? The worker design doc states that codalab.stanford.edu is based on TORQUE(correct me if I am wrong, I assume it is a shared grid computing environment)

---
Right now, we have a dedicated node to run webapp, REST api and bundle server. We just want to move workers to the nodes in grid.
Our grid has Portable Batch System (PBS) where I request (CPU and GPU) resources using `qsub`. 


1. Any suggestions/thoughts on how to make workers run in a distributed fashion, on-demand (i.e. request resources from grid's job scheduler, launch worker, and release the resources back when job is done). [Seems like the codalab.stanford.edu has a similar deployment](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/wiki/worker-design.pdf) . 
2. Additionally, I want to do run the jobs without using Docker (because we are unhappy about the [docker daemon attack surface](https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/security/#docker-daemon-attack-surface)). We have a shared FS that has same view across grid nodes (so download, upload not needed). I wont have too many different kinds of pipelines or worksheet, so, worksheets can assume all requirements are pre-setup. This is for the future step, doing step 1 is necessary for now, but just showing what might happen next.

Thanks,

issue_title: Some functions in nifgen that have repeated capabilities aren't generated properly
issue_body:
nifgen has script triggers and marker events, and the generator doesn't by default catch those inputs as repeated capabilities, so the methods are generated incorrectly.

Example: `configure_digital_level_script_trigger(self, trigger_id, source, trigger_when)`... trigger_id is repeated capability so the usage should be:

``` python
session.ScriptTriggers[0].configure_digital_level_script_trigger(self, source, trigger_when)
```

We didn't catch this in the system test... it is:

src/nifgen/system_tests/test_system_nifgen.py:    
``` python
session.configure_digital_level_script_trigger('ScriptTrigger0', 'PXI_Trig0', nifgen.TriggerWhen.HIGH)
```

This may go away depending what we do about #827

issue_title: Beams need to take rests/non-beam into account
issue_body:
Nitzan Ravhon reports:

from music21 import *

m = stream.Measure()
m.timeSignature = meter.TimeSignature('4/4')
for i in range(4):
    m.append(note.Rest('c4', quarterLength = 0.25))
    m.repeatAppend(note.Note('c4', quarterLength = 0.25), 3)
m2 = m.makeBeams()

beams are wrong for the first sixteenth after each rest -- TimeSignature.getBeams() does not take into account whether the duration is beamable or not.  a problem.

issue_title: None filtered/unrequested scenarios by tags are shown in report as "Skipped" if they have an attachment.
issue_body:
#### I'm submitting a ... 
  - [X] bug report


#### What is the current behavior?
When I run tests filtered by tags I see not selected scenarios as skipped if some steps in scenario has attachment, and this step reused in selected scenarios.

#### If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce and if possible a minimal demo of the problem
1. Create step that will have allure.attach usage
2. Create feature (for example: feature_1.feature) with 2 scenarios that use the step we created in step 1.
3. Tag one of the scenarios with a tag (for example: @tag1) and **don't** tag the second scenario
4. Now you should have file called feature_1.feature that looks something like this:
`Feature: Feature 1
  
@tag1
  Scenario: Scenario 1
    Given step

  Scenario: Scenario 2
    Given step

5. execute `behave -f allure_behave.formatter:AllureFormatter features/feature_1.feature --tags=tag1 `

#### What is the expected behavior?
I expect to see Scenario 1 in the report with an attachment and Scenario 2 not shown at all

#### What is the motivation / use case for changing the behavior?
I see Scenario 1 as passed with attachment on "Given step" and I see Scenario 2 as skipped without any attachment.

#### Please tell us about your environment:

| Allure version | 2.2.1 |
| Test framework | behave@1.2.5 |
| Allure adaptor | allure-python-commons=@2.2.1b1 |
| Generate report using | Allure Jenkins Plugin@2.22 |

issue_title: Compatibility with other Python versions
issue_body:
My team and I need to be able to use this library in Python 3.6, so I've begun testing against that.

In the process, however, I found that the tests are currently only passing against 2.7; they are not even passing currently against 2.6 or pypy2. Interestingly, it appears that Travis is no longer building this project, so this was never exposed.

I have begun the process of fixing the compatibility, which will likely result in a number of pull requests in order to make them smaller and more easily consumable.

issue_title: request: Custom 502 error page on per-application basis
issue_body:
I would like to replace the current default 502 Bad gateway page with a custom page on a per application basis.

*The use case is as follows:*
- Whenever we do a `git push deis` and update the application, in the transistion period between two versions where the old version is shutted down and the new version is started up, the page is replaced with a "502 Bad Gateway" nginx page. Instead of this page it would be useful to replace it with a custom HTML page (similar to what, for instance, Twitter and Reddit has) until the server is up and running again.

Ideally, it shouldn't be needed, but until #1829 rolling version update is added, I think this is a good solution.

I'm thinking it should be possible to upload a single html file. If the html file needs external resources, then that should either be stored on an external CDN or embedded in the page (base64).

issue_title: git push hangs on Azure when deploying an app if it takes > ~10 min
issue_body:
Hi all

I'm testing deis on Microsoft Azure.
The first time I create and deploy an app it usually takes > 10 min.
For example, when I create and deploy deis/example-php it takes ~15 min (in this case controller, builder and publisher are Standard_D1 VMs).
Deployment is successful, however git push always hangs, in my case at "-----> Pushing image to private registry".
In Azure you can set loadbalancer idle timeout and even public ip idle timeout as high as 30 min (see https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/virtual-networks-load-balancer-manage-idle-timeout/ ); however this is not solving the problem.
So I've done some tests.
From outside the cluster, git push hangs pushing both at port 2222 and 2223 (first case reaching nginx, second reaching the builder directly).
From inside the cluster, after creating an app, I changed the remote branch url and set it to the local address of the builder (at port 2223) or nginx (at port 2222) and then git push just works without hanging. 
From inside the cluster pushing at the public ip hangs in both cases. 

When sniffing with Wireshark the connection seems to be alive; it is not closed, however no traffic is generated until I CTRL-C git push and the connection is obviously closed.

Can you please help me found out what the problem is?
Isn't it worth to implement some sort of application level keep alive, and possibly have more precise progress status?
Possibly related: #3755.
Can you also post some benchmark or estimated time it takes to push deis sample apps in various aws instances so I can compare?

Thanks

issue_title: 'stateless plx' option
issue_body:
I've been running deis now for about three months with fairly frequent releases, in a DigitalOcean environment. I've had three major outages due to Deis:

 - The first cluster I put up became catatonic after etcd blew up and couldn't be saved (still on a version that's vulnerable)
 - The load balancer stopped forwarding to apps
 - The upgrade to 1.5.2 took about 30 minutes to complete -- too long imo. Should be sub-second outage.

I'm currently experiencing a third partial outage due to the drives on the builder machine having been filled up. The docs helped me free 400 MiB, but that's not enough, so I can't make progress (I've been waiting about an hour on `du` to finish, and meanwhile just using du crashed docker and that made deisctl fail completely (SSH timeout)).

I don't particularly like operating this fire service and I need to be sure that my customers can get to their apps.

I have these invariants:

 - I don't care about old releases much, most of the time. If I do, it's normally a rollback.
 - I don't want to have to manually clean disks - the deis platform should not roll itself over the edge of the cliff unless I allow it explicitly, but that's what it's currently doing
 - I don't care about saving old logs on disk -- I want to ship logs and I've set up papertrail - but I would much prefer semantic logging over "text" logging - i.e. Logentries or [Logary](https://logary.github.io/). I could take potentially ownership of this as I've written logary and have a fairly good idea of how to deal with logs.

Therefore I would suggest a 'stateless plx' option:

 - `deis releases prune` - remove all but the latest and also remove all docker containers not in use
 - no systemctl logs saved to disk by default - make ceph an add-on and expose it with *service discovery* for my apps instead
 - supply a default log router (Heka or Logstash) with op-/user-facing, mutable-after-deploy configuration, and configure it BOTH for metrics and for logs, *semantically* so that you don't have to parse the line of log in every log sink
 - supply a default alerting framework out of the box so I know when the platform is going belly-up -- and ensure log-levels and tags are used throughout the code-base: { verbose, debug, info, error, fatal } are my normal levels and fatal errors I always send text messages from -- I can write a logary target for 46elks and have texts available to deis users
 - supply a default backup engine such as [bacula](http://blog.bacula.org/) and have the CLI that configures the platform take a S3 API endpoint + credentials + encryption phrase
 - supply an inverse/corresponding 'provision from backup' command

issue_title: Look into moving JWT support to PyJWT; consider whether or not we can make it a strict dependency (vs. optional)
issue_body:
For JWT support, we allow users to install python-jose via the `globus-sdk[jwt]` extra. That makes the inclusion optional.
We made it optional because python-jose depends on pycrypto, which doesn't install cleanly without system libraries.

pyjwt is more mainstream than python-jose, and depends on `cryptography`. Among the many nice things about `cryptography` over `pycrypto`, it ships statically linked wheels for many platforms.

This means that a transition could do a couple of things for us:
- Improve the security of the tools we're implicitly endorsing, moving from `pycrypto` (which is dead) to `cryptography`
- Use deps more aligned with the CLI (which requires `cryptography` for delegate proxy)
- Possibly turn `jwt` support on by default, making `cryptography` a required dependency
  - This might work on our target platforms due to the static wheels as a format
  - We would need to expand our installation documentation to include notes on installing on the rare, unsupported platforms for `cryptography`
    e.g. `sudo apt-get install build-essential libssl-dev libffi-dev python3-dev`

However, this inherently makes `globus-sdk` installation more prone to failure. We're relying upon `cryptography` to retain good support for our target platforms, and if a platform is missing we expose a lot of extra setup burden.
We need to be *very confident* before pursuing this.

issue_title: itsdb.TestSuite.write() documentation doesn't match implementation
issue_body:
The documentation for `itsdb.TestSuite.write()` says its first argument is "an iterable of names of tables to write", but it in fact expects a mapping of table names to iterables of Records, or to `None`, which uses the stored records for the table.

It would be convenient and not terribly hard to accept several argument types:

```python
ts = TestSuite(...)
# all tables
ts.write()
# just the item table
ts.write('item')
# the item and parse tables
ts.write(['item', 'parse'])
# write item_rows to item, and the stored parse records to parse
ts.write({'item': item_rows, 'parse': None})
```

The docstring also needs to be updated to be correct WRT the implementation.

issue_title: Getting TDL cons- and diff-lists
issue_body:
Currently, getting the items from a list in TDL is rather inconvenient. E.g., for the orthography tokens from a lexical entry: `entry['ORTH.FIRST']`, `entry['ORTH.REST.FIRST']`, etc.

It would be more convenient to have a function to extract these to Pythonic lists. Here's such a function for cons lists:

```python
def extract_cons_list(d):
    if d is None: return []
    items = [d['FIRST']]
    items.extend(extract_cons_list(d['REST']))
    return items
```

issue_title: Dev docs
issue_body:
Conda doesn't use ReatTheDoc which is fine - I assume there are good reasons for it, however the conda docs seem to be missing some features as compared to RTD.

Specifically the docs don't seem to be tied to any particular version of `conda`. I assume that [https://conda.io/docs/](https://conda.io/docs/) always just points to the latest stable release, but sometimes you want to read up on new features in unreleased versions, or to actually find the docs for the specific version you're currently running which may not always be the latest stable.

RTD allows you to view the docs for any past version and for the upcoming, unreleased version which is a *very* useful feature. It would be good if the conda docs allowed you to do the same. The most important aspect for me is to be able to (easily) read up about the new features which have been developed but not yet released.

Is that actually correct that those docs aren't published or have I just missed them somewhere?

issue_title: Multiple installed packages
issue_body:
Not sure if this option is on purpose: but it is possible to install multiple packages with the same name and version:
```
workerm@notebookpc:~/0__PythonCodingEnviroment__0/bin$ ./conda list
# packages in environment at /home/workerm/0__PythonCodingEnviroment__0:
#
appdirs                   1.2.0           build_on_debian_wheezy__p33

see: appdirs and conda

obviously they overwrite each others files (if they have the same file):
I know there is the option:

but should that not replace the package with the same name and version: instead ending up with 3 packages like I did with conda?

issue_title: Conda (small) bug under windows, searching for a badly named executable/script
issue_body:
With the install on Windows 10:

  _platform : win-64   conda version : 4.3.30
  conda is private : False   conda-env version : 4.3.30
  conda-build version : 3.0.22   python version : 3.6.3.final.0
  requests version : 2.18.4     root environment : C:\Anaconda3  (writable)_

the command:  `conda help install`  (or other similar, say, conda help create, etc.) produces the message

`C:\Anaconda3\python.exe: can't open file 'C:\Anaconda3\Scripts\conda': [Errno 2] No such file or directory`

This syntax works under Linux (Ubuntu), but of course, there is no just "conda" file in Windows Anaconda Scripts.

issue_title: "root" environment needs a different name
issue_body:
`/root` is what the lay user will think the "_root environment_" refers to.  _root_ is fairly universally assumed to mean _things associated with the root super user_.

I very strongly believe, even though it is late in the day, we need to rename _root environment_ to something like _base environment_ that will not create this confusion.

issue_title: 4.3.0 beta released
issue_body:
Hello,

I've just released a beta version with all the fixes for the bug reported by @Astalaseven  and @Bofinken. The beta also includes experimental support for SQL Cobol on Linux and for using a custom GnuCobol compiler on Windows.

Installer for Windows and Mac OSX are available in the [release section on github](https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobolIDE/releases/tag/4.3.0b).

Linux users need to upgrade OpenCobolIDE and pyQode from source checkout. This can easily be done by running the following commands:

```
sudo pip3 install git+https://github.com/pyQode/pyqode.qt.git --upgrade
sudo pip3 install git+https://github.com/pyQode/pyqode.core.git --upgrade
sudo pip3 install git+https://github.com/pyQode/pyqode.cobol.git --upgrade
sudo pip3 install git+https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobolIDE.git --upgrade
```

I already updated the documentation. There are 3 new topics:

- [a description of the preferences settings](http://opencobolide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/settings.html)
- [a guide on how to use a custom compiler on windows](http://opencobolide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/advanced.html#how-to-use-a-custom-gnucobol-compiler-build-on-windows)
- [a guide on how to setup dbpre integration on linux](http://opencobolide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/advanced.html#how-to-setup-dbpre-integration-with-opencobolide)

@Bofinken, @Astalaseven, @LuizFlavio, @martyheyman, @abbhishek Please give it a try! Feel free to give your feedback in this thread!

The final release is planned for the 11th January 2015.

issue_title: Adding a tag to a resource is unacceptably slow
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
Running 'az resource tag --id ID --tags KEY=VALUE' or running the equivalent update operation (e.g. 'az network public-ip update --tags') consistently takes just over 30 seconds

**To Reproduce**
Run 'az resource tag --id ID --tags KEY=VALUE' and time the command. It will take just over 30 seconds.

**Expected behavior**
Adding a tag to a resource through the Azure portal takes a few seconds. The Azure CLI shouldn't be significantly (10x) slower.

**Environment summary**
Azure CLI 2.0.33 on both Windows and Linux 

**Additional context**
Running with the '--debug' operation suggests the following. First, the CLI issues several GET and POSTS requests to Azure to tell Azure to update the resource's tags. Then the CLI waits for 30 seconds (I assume waiting for Azure to complete the operation). Then the CLI issues several GET requests to Azure (I assume to see if the tag operation was performed successfully). Is there a way to configure the 30 second wait because it seems like it could be shortened significantly?

issue_title: Out of memory when running az storage blob upload
issue_body:
I'm using az to upload an image to azure.
I'm using python3, but have seen the same issue with python2.
Image is 30G (although only 1,5 G sparse).
Eventually I managed to upload using --max-connections 1.
I traced the issue down to this little piece of code:
file: storage/blob/_upload_chunking.py, line:70
``` python
    if max_connections > 1:
        import concurrent.futures
        executor = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_connections)
        range_ids = list(executor.map(uploader.process_chunk, uploader.get_chunk_streams()))
    else:
        range_ids = [uploader.process_chunk(result) for result in uploader.get_chunk_streams()]
```
Problem is with the line
``` python
        range_ids = list(executor.map(uploader.process_chunk, uploader.get_chunk_streams()))
```
calling executor.map with parameter uploader.get_chunk_streams() creates a list with all the element that are yielded in get_chunk_streams().
This list holds all 30G of file data and is built in memory before passing it on to executor.map().

So, if you want to upload with maxconnections > 1, basically, you need at least (memory+swap) larger than file you wish to upload...

issue_title: az login --service-principal does not update azureProfile.json with changed password
issue_body:
The following steps lead to a not very telling error message:

1) Create a service principal using `az ad sp create`
2) Log in using that service principal (`az login --service-principal <...>`)
3) Do something, e.g. `az group list` to verify it works
4) Add a new password, e.g. using the Azure Portal
5) Log in using the new password (again, using `az login --service-principal`) --> This will succeed
6) Try some other command, e.g. again `az group list`

Result:

```
Get Token request returned http error: 401 and server response: {"error":"invalid_client","error_description":"AADSTS70002: Error validating credentials. AADSTS50012: Invalid client secret is provided.\r\nTrace ID: xxxxxxxx-c335-41d9-83cb-16455a550c00\r\nCorrelation ID: xxxxxxxx-604c-47f4-96d0-d8acf0e78513\r\nTimestamp: 2018-03-21 19:30:18Z","error_codes":[70002,50012],"timestamp":"2018-03-21 19:30:18Z","trace_id":"xxxxxxxx-c335-41d9-83cb-16455a550c00","correlation_id":"xxxxxxxx-604c-47f4-xxxx-d8acf0e78513"}
```

The issue seems to be that the password is persisted in `~/.azure/azureProfile.json` (as `accessToken` inside the JSON), but doesn't seem to be updated if a new password is given. The `az login` succeeds with the new password, but subsequent commands still pull the old password from the `azureProfile.json`.

This took us a while to figure out.

---

### Environment summary

Verified behaviour on 2.0.27 to 2.0.29, but probably is and has always been like this. We have seen it on macOS and Ubuntu machines.

issue_title: add support for ARM template parameters in properties format
issue_body:
ARM template deployment accepts parameters only in JSON format, see  https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/resource-group-template-deploy
The parameter file must be in the following format:
```
 {
   "$schema": "https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2015-01-01/deploymentParameters.json#",
   "contentVersion": "1.0.0.0",
   "parameters": {
      "storageNamePrefix": {
          "value": "contoso"
      },
      "storageSKU": {
          "value": "Standard_GRS"
      }
   }
 }
```
However, maintaining config params in JSON format has following disadvantages:

1. Lack of comments. One can’t add comments in JSON files. This is very problematic, because:
 - No way to document on why settings were set to what they are.
 - No way to add mnemonics or describe caveats.
 - No way to keep a basic ChangeLog in the config file itself, documenting which changes were made (which can be immensely useful)
2. Much more difficult to debug, as quickly commenting out a section isn’t possible.
3. Readability. JSON is readable for a data-interchange format, but not readable for a configuration file.
4. Strictness.The JSON standard is fairly strict, it means it’s more difficult to write. For example a trailing comma in objects or arrays is an error.

Please, add support for simple properties files for params, e.g
```
storageNamePrefix=contoso
storageSKU=Standard_GRS
```
